references.name: Exodus 24 (Moses on Mount Sinai)
references.context: God instructs Moses to come up to the mountain to receive tablets inscribed with the law and commandments for God's people to know how to live in covenant with God.

references.name: Exodus 19:9, 24:15-18 (Pillar of cloud)
references.context: God leads the Israelites through the desert as a pillar of cloud by day, establishing cloud imagery throughout Hebrew Scripture.

references.name: Isaiah 19:1 (Isaiah foretelling God on a swift cloud)
references.context: Isaiah foretells the coming of the Lord on a swift cloud to judge the nations, demonstrating cloud imagery in prophetic literature.

references.name: Acts 1:9 (Jesus ascends in a cloud)
references.context: Jesus ascends in a cloud, establishing cloud imagery in the New Testament narrative.

references.name: Mark 14:62 (Return with clouds)
references.context: It is said that Jesus will return with the clouds, connecting eschatological hope to cloud imagery.

references.name: Revelation 1:7 (Return with clouds)
references.context: Jesus will return with the clouds according to Revelation, reinforcing apocalyptic cloud imagery.

references.name: Genesis 1-2 (Creation story, seventh day)
references.context: Moses acclimates for six days, and like the creation story, God calls Moses out of the cloud on the seventh day, creating a structural parallel.

references.name: Matthew 17:1-8 (Transfiguration of Jesus)
references.context: The transfiguration story is paired with the Exodus narrative to help hearers recognize echoes in Scripture—Moses, Elijah, and Jesus all encountered God on mountaintops with God appearing as a cloud.

references.name: 1 Kings 19:11-13 (Elijah and the still, small voice)
references.context: Elijah had a mountaintop experience where he heard God's voice as a still, small voice or whisper, paired with Moses as a mountaintop figure.

references.name: Matthew 3:17 (Jesus' baptism)
references.context: God repeats what was said at Jesus' baptism—'This is my Son, the Beloved, with whom I am well pleased'—but adds 'Listen to him' as an echo of the commandments.

references.name: John 13:34 (New commandment to love)
references.context: Jesus gives a new commandment: 'that you love one another just as I have loved you,' echoing the original commandments given to Moses.

references.name: Matthew 17:4 (Peter's offer to build tabernacles)
references.context: Peter offered to keep the mountaintop experience alive by building tabernacles, but God does not keep the faithful men on the mountain.

references.name: Matthew 17:1-8; Mark 9:2-8; Luke 9:28-36 (Transfiguration with Peter, James, and John)
references.context: Peter, James, and John witness the transfiguration and are sent back down to do God's work in the world, mirroring the pattern of descent after mountaintop encounters.

references.name: Liturgical Season of Epiphany (Christmas through Lent)
references.context: The season after Epiphany is described as weeks between Christmas and Lent where God's manifestation, incarnation, and revelation are learned about—a liturgical mountaintop experience.

references.name: Season of Lent
references.context: Lent is presented as a time of literal and figurative grounding and reflection following the mountaintop season, mirroring the physical descent from the mountain.

references.name: Cloud of Unknowing / Apophatic theology / Cloud mysticism
references.context: The extended meditation on encountering God as cloud—mysterious, intangible, enshrouding, ominous and whimsical—echoes medieval mystical traditions of encountering the divine through obscurity and unknowing.

references.name: Mount Everest climbing metaphor
references.context: Moses is compared to 'a climber on Everest' who must acclimate at base camp before ascending, using modern mountaineering as an analogy for spiritual preparation.

references.name: Colorado 14ers (mountain peaks)
references.context: The preacher uses the specific geographical experience of Colorado mountains and afternoon lightning storms to help the congregation viscerally imagine the terror of the Israelites witnessing God as fire on the mountain.

primary_theme: God encounters us as a cloud—mysterious, protective, fearsome, and intimate—calling us to mountaintop experiences of divine encounter and commissioning us to descend and love one another in the world.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling, grounded in personal experience (moving to Colorado) and local geography, employing typological biblical interpretation to connect Old Testament Exodus narrative with New Testament Transfiguration, interspersed with personal theological questioning and liturgical catechesis.

references.name: Perry Mason (TV series)
references.context: Preacher recalls watching Perry Mason as a child sick from school, loving 'the buildup in the courtroom, the drama of the always innocent client being saved by the investigative prowess of Paul Drake, and the legal brilliance of Perry Mason.'

references.name: Raymond Burr
references.context: Preacher notes she 'grew up thinking I wanted to be a lawyer because of Raymond Burr's portrayal' of Perry Mason.

references.name: Book of Micah
references.context: Central biblical text for the sermon; preacher describes it as 'a courtroom drama' and the primary source for the sermon's ethical teaching.

references.name: Micah 6:6-8
references.context: The crescendo passage: 'He told you, O mortal, what is good. And what does the Lord require of you but to do justice and to love kindness and to walk humbly with your God?'

references.name: Hebrew Scripture (general)
references.context: Preacher describes Micah 6:6-8 as 'perhaps one of the most recognizable passages from all of Hebrew scripture.'

references.name: Biblical Ethics
references.context: Preacher cites 'Many scholars call this verdict biblical ethics in a nutshell,' referring to Micah's teaching on justice, kindness, and humility.

references.name: The Beatitudes
references.context: Preacher references Jesus's teaching: 'He teaches that the meek, those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, the merciful, and the peacemakers are all already blessed.'

references.name: Matthew 5:3-10 (Beatitudes)
references.context: Jesus's teaching on blessedness cited as an expansion of Micah's biblical ethic.

references.name: Anne Lamott
references.context: Preacher cites her as 'one of our modern-day prophets' and quotes: 'we think we're hungry for what we're not getting, when in reality, we're hungry for what we're not giving.'

references.name: The Lectionary
references.context: Preacher reflects on 'how the lectionary on a three-year rotation...can speak so profoundly and prophetically into our present day,' describing it as 'a gift wrapped in thousands of years of human experience and assembled by a committee.'

references.name: Covenant theology
references.context: Preacher discusses how 'the people who have reduced their relationship with God to one of transaction' have broken covenant, and that 'right relationship with God' is the restored covenant bond.

references.name: Prophetic tradition
references.context: Preacher positions both Micah and Anne Lamott within the prophetic tradition, emphasizing prophetic critique of systems and call to justice.

references.name: The concept of grace
references.context: Preacher teaches that 'the response to the grace and salvation we have been shown is to want to share that with others' and that 'the grace that God has poured into them is issuing out of them and into the world.'

references.name: Prophetic justice tradition
references.context: The sermon invokes the prophetic critique of systems of oppression: 'what can you do when you are feeling small and helpless against oppressive systems that seem intent on crushing the least of these?'

primary_theme: Biblical ethics—summed up in Micah's teaching to 'do justice, love kindness, and walk humbly with God'—requires active engagement in justice and relationship with both God and neighbor, not transactional religious performance or passive piety.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-driven pastoral reflection with prophetic challenge; uses extended courtroom drama metaphor (Perry Mason analogy) to make biblical narrative vivid and contemporary, grounded in personal anecdote and modern prophetic voice (Anne Lamott), culminating in pastoral exhortation to embodied Christian living.

references.name: Genesis 3
references.context: The Lord God walking in the garden at the time of the evening breeze, then calling out to Adam and Eve, 'Where are you?'

references.name: Matthew 27
references.context: Jesus crying out at about three o'clock: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'

references.name: Isaiah 49
references.context: The Lord called me before I was born; while I was in my mother's womb, he named me.

references.name: John 1 (Gospel reading)
references.context: Jesus's first recorded question to his disciples: 'What are you looking for?' and invitation to 'come and see'; contains 10+ words related to sight (seeing, looking, watching, revealing).

references.name: Jesus's teaching formula
references.context: Preacher notes Jesus begins statements with 'Very truly I tell you' as a signal to pay attention.

references.name: Baptismal vows
references.context: Referenced through confirmation class where teens seek to confirm vows made on their behalf at baptism.

references.name: Catechumenate tradition
references.context: Adults going through catechumenate class (21 mentioned) receiving sacrament at Easter vigil, seeking Christian identity.

references.name: Episcopal Church practice
references.context: Reference to what it means to be Episcopalian and classes on Anglican practice.

references.name: Flannery O'Connor
references.context: O'Connor quoted for her writing discipline: 'Every morning between 9 and 12, I go to my room and sit before a piece of paper... If an idea does come between 9 and 12, I'm ready for it.'

references.name: Sarah Strand
references.context: Youth minister cited for writing about existential questions regarding confirmation class in The Voice publication.

references.name: The Voice (publication)
references.context: Church publication where Sarah Strand wrote about existential questions related to confirmation.

references.name: Existentialism / Existential questions
references.context: Preacher describes the sermon's central questions ('What are you looking for?') and confirmation themes as 'big existential questions and wonderings.'

references.name: Mysticism / Contemplative practice
references.context: Implicit in the emphasis on spiritual seeking, divine presence through time, and the practice of sitting in readiness for encounter.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: Implicit throughout the sermon's emphasis on God's embodied presence, movement through time and space, and divine closeness ('the divine is oh so close').

primary_theme: The spiritual practice of seeking Jesus is a lifelong, evolving journey that requires discipline, openness, and attention to God's specific, intimate presence in time and in our lives.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining theological meditation on scripture, personal spiritual testimony, literary illustration (O'Connor), and gentle prophetic challenge to cultivate spiritual discipline and curiosity.

references.name: Henry Nouwen
references.context: Preacher quotes Nouwen's 'The Road to Peace' to explain that God incarnate as a baby requires human care and teaching: 'Just as God cares for us, it's very important that we care for God in the world.'

references.name: The Road to Peace
references.context: Nouwen's work is cited as the source for theological reflection on God's dependence on human beings for growth and care.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The nativity narrative is the primary scriptural foundation, including the shepherds, angels, and Mary's pondering: 'all who heard it were amazed at what the shepherds told them, and Mary treasured and pondered them in her heart' (Luke 2).

references.name: Luke 2 (Nativity account with shepherds and angels)
references.context: Extended reference to the shepherds' encounter with the heavenly host and their visit to the newborn Jesus.

references.name: Gabriel (Angel)
references.context: Referenced as the angel who appeared to Mary nine months before the nativity with the message that 'The child to be born will be holy. He will be called the son of God.'

references.name: Elizabeth (Mary's cousin)
references.context: Quoted as affirming Mary's pregnancy: 'Blessed is the fruit of your womb' and expressing wonder that 'the mother of the Lord would come to her.'

references.name: The Magnificat
references.context: Referenced as Mary's canticle during her visit to Elizabeth, where she speaks 'of the glory, the disruption, the mercy, and the compassion of God.'

references.name: Emmanuel
references.context: The theological concept of 'God with us' is invoked multiple times as the central meaning of the incarnation: 'Emmanuel, Emmanuel...God incarnate.'

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The sermon is built on the theological tradition that God becomes human, vulnerable, and dependent, requiring human care and nurture.

references.name: Nouwen's concept of divine weakness and human response
references.context: The idea that God becomes 'weak' in the manger to invite human love and care reflects Nouwen's mystical theology of divine vulnerability.

references.name: Christian pastoral theology of parenting and discipleship
references.context: The sermon draws on pastoral theology's understanding of parental care as analogous to spiritual formation and discipleship.

primary_theme: The Incarnation invites Christians to recognize God's vulnerability and dependence in Jesus and to respond by embodying divine values of mercy, compassion, and care toward one another in their daily lives after Christmas.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses personal parenting experience, imaginative meditation on Mary's perspective, and theological quotation to move from scriptural affirmation to ethical application.

references.name: Edward Hicks
references.context: Self-taught Quaker artist (mid-1800s, Philadelphia) who painted 'The Peaceable Kingdom' 62 times, with versions in major museums including Denver Art Museum, Met, Smithsonian, and DeYoung.

references.name: The Peaceable Kingdom
references.context: Painting series by Edward Hicks based on Isaiah 11, depicting animals of predator and prey coexisting in harmony; Hicks painted variations 62 times.

references.name: Isaiah (Prophet)
references.context: Prophet whose vision of harmony between predator and prey animals serves as the primary theological foundation for the sermon's meditation on peace.

references.name: Isaiah 11
references.context: The primary biblical source describing the peaceable kingdom where 'the wolf shall live with the lamb' and predator and prey coexist in harmony.

references.name: Isaiah 65:19-25
references.context: Quoted passage describing God's new creation: 'No more shall the sound of weeping be heard...infant mortality will be a thing of the past, and people will live long, healthy lives.'

references.name: Isaiah 65:24
references.context: Quoted twice: 'Before they call, I will answer. While they are yet speaking, I will hear.' Used to develop the theme of God's present-time attentiveness to human suffering.

references.name: William Blake
references.context: Poet and artist cited as one who has 'played with this image [of the peaceable kingdom] in art and theology and music' alongside Stanley Hauerwas and Patti Smith.

references.name: Stanley Hauerwas
references.context: Theologian cited as one who has engaged with the peaceable kingdom image in theology, alongside William Blake and Patti Smith.

references.name: Patti Smith
references.context: Artist/musician cited as one who has 'played with this image [of the peaceable kingdom] in art and theology and music.'

references.name: Eden
references.context: Alluded to indirectly as preacher describes Isaiah's hope for 'an Eden-esque time where God's people lived in harmony with the natural world.'

references.name: William Penn and the Lenape Treaty
references.context: Referenced as a secondary subject in Edward Hicks' peaceable kingdom paintings, depicting human reconciliation and peace treaties.

references.name: Quaker tradition/theology
references.context: Edward Hicks identified as a Quaker artist; the tradition implicitly valued in the sermon's emphasis on peace and non-violence; Quaker schism referenced as subject in Hicks paintings.

references.name: Eschatological theology (now but not yet)
references.context: The sermonic framework distinguishing between present reality and future fulfillment: 'this now-but-not-yet time...that new heaven and new earth, they're not here yet.'

references.name: Liberation theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on God hearing the oppressed, restoring the exiled, and the promise that the oppressed 'shall not imitate the oppressor' echoes liberation theology's concern with God's preferential option for the marginalized.

references.name: Prophetic justice tradition (implicit)
references.context: The sermon invokes the prophetic tradition of speaking comfort to the exiled and oppressed, promising divine reversal: 'God says, no more' to oppression and suffering.

primary_theme: In the 'now-but-not-yet' time between present suffering and future redemption, God offers the immediate comfort of being heard and calls believers to practice peaceable coexistence and non-violent reconciliation across differences, modeling the harmony depicted in Isaiah's vision of the peaceable kingdom.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral meditation grounded in visual art and scripture; the preacher uses personal narrative (hospital visit to sick friend), extended reflection on a painting series, and close exegetical reading of Isaiah to develop a theology of present-time divine attentiveness and future peace. The style is contemplative and invitational rather than argumentative, asking rhetorical questions to draw listeners into imaginative participation in the vision.

references.name: Gospel of John (John 1)
references.context: The entire sermon's biblical foundation, centering on John 1:35-51 where Jesus meets Nathaniel through Philip's invitation.

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Points to Jesus as 'the Lamb of God' in John 1, initiating the chain of witness that goes 'viral person to person.'

references.name: Andrew
references.context: Follows John the Baptist's testimony and tells his brother Simon Peter about Jesus, demonstrating viral faith transmission.

references.name: Simon Peter
references.context: Called by Andrew after Andrew witnesses John the Baptist point to Jesus.

references.name: Philip
references.context: Found by Jesus and then evangelizes Nathaniel with the invitation 'Come and see,' central to the sermon's argument about invitation-based faith.

references.name: Nathaniel
references.context: Skeptical at first ('Can anything good come out of Nazareth?'), then sees Jesus and believes, exemplifying the sermon's pattern of viral faith.

references.name: Jesus Christ
references.context: Sees Nathaniel under the fig tree before Philip called him, demonstrating intimate divine knowing and catalyzing Nathaniel's faith.

references.name: Moses
references.context: Referenced in Nathaniel's testimony that Jesus is 'he about whom Moses in the law and also the prophets wrote.'

references.name: Nicodemus
references.context: Listed as an example from John's gospel of skeptics who 'see and believe' and then tell others.

references.name: Woman at the Well
references.context: Cited as an example from John's gospel of someone who sees, believes, and spreads the faith.

references.name: Thomas
references.context: Listed as an example of a skeptical character in John who eventually sees and believes.

references.name: Margaret Atwood
references.context: Canadian author and poet who shared and praised the viral 'cat lawyer' video, adding her own witty caption: 'I on the other hand am a cat. I just can't get this human filter off.'

references.name: Thomas Cranmer
references.context: Translated the Collect for Purity from 10th-century Latin into English; the prayer is 'still prayed around the world weekly.'

references.name: Collect for Purity
references.context: A 10th-century liturgical prayer translated by Thomas Cranmer expressing God's intimate knowledge: 'Almighty God, to you all hearts are open, all desires known, and from you no secrets are hid.'

references.name: The 'cat lawyer' viral video (Zoom filter incident)
references.context: Used as a contemporary example of how something becomes 'viral' in modern culture, paralleling how faith spreads person-to-person.

references.name: Johannine theology of 'seeing' as faith
references.context: The sermon emphasizes that 'seeing is a key theme throughout this entire gospel' and that 'true seeing involves a spiritual revelation of Jesus as the divine son of God, leading to belief and faith.'

primary_theme: The Christian faith spreads 'virally' through personal invitation and witness—the 'Come and see' model—where direct encounter with Jesus (or testimony about Him) leads to belief, as exemplified in John 1's chain of disciples.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection; opens with a contemporary viral video anecdote, transitions to biblical narrative, then applies the biblical pattern to the congregation's own witness, closing with reflective questions that invite personal engagement.

references.name: Book of Jeremiah
references.context: The sermon's primary text; Pearson focuses on Jeremiah's lament passages expressing grief and despair about the people's spiritual condition.

references.name: Jeremiah (the prophet) / 'the weeping prophet'
references.context: Pearson invokes Jeremiah as a prophetic voice whose words about spiritual unfaithfulness remain relevant to contemporary life.

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Described as 'Episcopal priest, author, and public theologian' and 'one of the wisest gifts of the Episcopal Church'; her extended reflection on disillusionment with false expectations of God is quoted at length.

references.name: Taylor, Barbara Brown (specific work/quote)
references.context: Pearson quotes Taylor's reflection beginning 'For those willing to keep heaving themselves toward the light...' through 'Every time God declines to meet my expectations, another of my idols is exposed.'

references.name: Jeremiah 8:20 (implied)
references.context: The lament 'The harvest is past. The summer is ended. And we are not saved' is quoted directly from Jeremiah.

references.name: Jeremiah 8:22 (implied)
references.context: The question 'Is there no balm in Gilead? Is there no physician there?' frames the sermon's discussion of healing and hope.

references.name: 'There Is a Balm in Gilead' (African American spiritual)
references.context: Pearson discusses how this spiritual transforms Jeremiah's lament question into an affirmative answer about healing and hope.

references.name: African American spiritual tradition
references.context: Pearson invokes the spiritual 'There Is a Balm in Gilead' as an example of how faith traditions transform despair into hope.

references.name: Pentecost
references.context: Referenced in the sermon title 'The Fifteenth Sunday after Pentecost,' marking the liturgical calendar position.

references.name: Biblical concept of idolatry
references.context: Pearson draws on the prophetic critique of idolatry from Jeremiah to analyze modern false expectations of God (phones, need to be right, cruelty, love of power).

references.name: Biblical concept of repentance
references.context: Pearson emphasizes Jeremiah's call to repentance and contrasts the Israelites' refusal to repent with the need for contemporary spiritual transformation.

references.name: Theological concept of 'partnership with God'
references.context: Pearson states 'We are partners with God' and emphasizes active engagement in 'repentance and restoration and kingdom building' rather than passive waiting.

references.name: Kingdom theology / 'kingdom building'
references.context: Pearson references 'kingdom building' as human participation in God's transformative work.

references.name: Holy Spirit
references.context: Referenced in the spiritual's line 'the Holy Spirit revives my soul again' as the source of spiritual renewal.

primary_theme: God's refusal to meet our false expectations (idolatry) invites us into deeper faith and partnership with God in healing and restoration.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection grounded in prophetic critique, combining scholarly textual discussion with contemporary application and liturgical-spiritual resources (spirituals, quoted theologian); conversational and invitational rather than didactic.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The entire sermon is grounded in Luke's Gospel, particularly Luke 1-2 and Luke 12, which the preacher argues consistently presents peace and division as two sides of the same coin.

references.name: Luke 1 - The Magnificat
references.context: Mary's song where she declares that Jesus will 'bring down the powerful and raise up the lowly' and 'fill the hungry with good things while sending the rich away empty.'

references.name: Luke 2 - Birth narrative and angelic proclamation
references.context: Angels announce 'glory to God in the highest and on earth, peace' at Jesus's birth, establishing the paradox of the 'Prince of Peace' who was 'always intended to cause trouble.'

references.name: Luke 2 - Simeon's prophecy
references.context: Simeon tells Mary that 'This child is destined for the falling and the rising of many in Israel, and a sword will pierce your own soul too.'

references.name: Luke 12 - Jesus's apocalyptic speech
references.context: Jesus declares 'I have come to bring fire to the earth and not peace whatsoever' and that 'division, households will be divided and family members will turn against one another.'

references.name: Matthew 18:22 - Forgiveness 70 times 7
references.context: Referenced as an example of Jesus's dramatic and hyperbolic rhetorical style that Christians accept as metaphorical.

references.name: Jesus's parables
references.context: Preacher notes that Jesus 'teaches in wild and often illogical parables' yet Christians insist on literal interpretation of his division sayings.

references.name: G. K. Chesterton
references.context: Described as 'Author, philosopher, and apologist' who wrote about Christ's literary style being 'gigantesque' and 'full of camels leaping through needles and mountains hurled into the sea.'

references.name: Chesterton quotation on Christ's literary style
references.context: Direct quote: 'Christ had even a literary style of his own. The diction used by Christ is quite curiously gigantesque. It is full of camels leaping through needles and mountains hurled into the sea.'

references.name: Pax Romana
references.context: Referenced as the historical period in which Luke's audience lived, where 'there was no real peace' despite the name, illustrating the gap between claimed peace and actual conditions.

references.name: Lectionary tradition
references.context: The sermon is framed within the liturgical calendar ('The Tenth Sunday after Pentecost') and the preacher mentions 'our lectionary study' where the community wrestled with the passage.

references.name: Episcopal theology/practice
references.context: The preacher identifies as Episcopal and references 'a very Episcopal move' of holding two things in tension, and mentions those being rejected for worshipping 'here in the Episcopal Church.'

references.name: Cherry-picking scripture
references.context: Preacher warns that 'any one of us can cherry pick a verse to justify our beliefs, our prejudices,' implicitly referencing the problem of selective biblical interpretation.

references.name: Burning bush - God's presence
references.context: Referenced as an example of fire in Scripture indicating divine presence: 'God appeared to Moses in a burning bush.'

references.name: Exodus - Pillar of fire
references.context: Cited as another scriptural example: 'God led the Israelites out of bondage as a pillar of fire by night.'

references.name: John the Baptist's baptism teaching
references.context: Referenced to show fire as purifying: 'John said that he baptizes with water, but Jesus will baptize with fire and the Holy Spirit.'

references.name: Pentecost - Tongues of fire
references.context: Cited as scriptural evidence that fire indicates divine presence: 'Tongues of fire descended on the disciples' heads at Pentecost.'

references.name: Kingdom of God theology
references.context: The preacher invites listeners to 'partner with the triune God in cultivating the kingdom of God here and now,' reflecting an already-not-yet eschatology.

primary_theme: Jesus's message necessarily causes division and disruption, but this should not be read as condemning family separation; rather, it invites Christians to work toward authentic peace through deep listening and bridge-building while recognizing that following Jesus has always been risky and transformative.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic challenge, using narrative storytelling of Luke's Gospel, biblical typology, direct address to personal pain, scholarly literary analysis (via Chesterton), and wrestling with contemporary theological tensions to move from acknowledging discomfort with the text to reframing it as invitation and opportunity.

references.name: Isaiah (Prophet)
references.context: Isaiah predicts the coming of a 'wonderful counselor, a mighty God, Prince of Peace.'

references.name: Isaiah 9:6
references.context: Reference to the prophecy of the Prince of Peace in Isaiah.

references.name: Genesis through Revelation
references.context: Peace is mentioned 'hundreds upon hundreds upon hundreds' of times throughout the entire biblical canon.

references.name: Luke 10:1-12 (implied)
references.context: The 72 disciples Jesus sends out in pairs with instructions to offer peace as their greeting; 'whatever house you enter, first say, peace to this house.'

references.name: Galatians 6:9
references.context: Paul's exhortation: 'Let us not grow weary of doing what is right, for we will reap at harvest time if we don't give up.'

references.name: Philippians 4:7 (implied)
references.context: Reference to 'the peace that passes all understanding,' prayed for particularly in times of crisis.

references.name: Babar
references.context: Children's book found on shelves in the family's Sewanee home, representing artifacts of childhood.

references.name: Richard Scarry
references.context: Children's author whose books are found on shelves in the family's Sewanee home, representing artifacts of childhood.

references.name: The University of the South, Sewanee
references.context: Referenced as an intellectual and spiritual institution where the preacher and her family have deep roots: met as undergrads, children attended, married in All Saints Chapel.

references.name: All Saints Chapel (Sewanee)
references.context: Historic chapel at Sewanee used as 'true north' for the Pearson family, embodying continuity and sacred space.

references.name: St. John's Cathedral (Denver)
references.context: The preacher's current institutional affiliation and context for the sermon; compared to All Saints Chapel in its spiritual significance.

references.name: Episcopal Church liturgical tradition
references.context: The passing of the peace in the liturgy is described as a weekly practice where 'we extend our hands to people, some we know and some we've never met.'

references.name: Christian hospitality tradition
references.context: The concept of welcoming strangers and offering peace as rooted in Christian practice and theology.

references.name: Kingdom of God theology
references.context: Peace is presented as 'a sign of the kingdom of God,' which 'came near to those who accepted it' and those who did not.

references.name: Practicing Christian identity
references.context: The term 'practicing Christians' is explored to suggest ongoing spiritual practice and formation in peace-making.

primary_theme: Offering God's peace to others—through greeting, hospitality, and the practice of the liturgical passing of peace—is the fundamental Christian vocation and a sign that the kingdom of God has drawn near, regardless of how it is received.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling grounded in personal testimony, combined with liturgical and scriptural meditation; the preacher uses family anecdotes and physical objects (the bronze plaque) as anchors for theological reflection, moving from the particular to the universal principle.

references.name: Psalm 145:3
references.context: "Great is the Lord and greatly to be praised, and his greatness is unsearchable."

references.name: Psalm 139:6
references.context: "Such knowledge is too wonderful for me. It is high. I cannot attain it."

references.name: Isaiah 55:8-9
references.context: "For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways, declares the Lord. For as the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than yours, and my thoughts than your thoughts."

references.name: 1 Corinthians 2:10-11
references.context: "The Spirit searches everything, even the depths of God, and no one comprehends the things of God except the Spirit of God."

references.name: John (contemporary text)
references.context: "The truth will be revealed to us when we can bear it" (attributed to Jesus in today's text from John).

references.name: Proverbs (reading by Karen)
references.context: Shows wisdom as a manifestation of God's own self and God's visibility through creation.

references.name: Winston Churchill
references.context: The Trinity is described as "a riddle wrapped in a mystery inside an enigma" (paraphrased from Churchill's description of Russian policy).

references.name: Ellen Davis
references.context: Duke Divinity professor of Old Testament cited as authority: "The Proverbs are spiritual guides for ordinary people."

references.name: Richard (likely Richard Bauknight, rector)
references.context: Cited for the observation that "Episcopalians have a high tolerance for ambiguity."

references.name: Mystery tradition / Apophatic theology
references.context: The sermon embraces the via negativa approach—emphasizing what cannot be said about God rather than attempting positive theological claims.

references.name: Anglican/Episcopal theological tradition
references.context: The embrace of ambiguity and comfort with theological mystery is presented as distinctively Anglican/Episcopal.

references.name: Cloud of Unknowing (implicit)
references.context: The medieval mystical tradition's concept of the unknowability of God and the limits of human language in approaching the divine.

primary_theme: The Trinity is an inexplicable mystery that invites awe and humility rather than intellectual comprehension, and God reveals himself through this mystery in ordinary creation and experience, calling us to know God rather than to understand doctrine.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with personal narrative; uses humor and self-deprecation, builds argument through scriptural authority and testimony to lived experience, culminates in prophetic invitation to contemplative practice.

references.name: Luke 24:4-6 (or parallel Gospel account)
references.context: Two men in white at the tomb ask women, 'Why do you look for the living among the dead? He is not here, but he has risen.'

references.name: Acts 1:10-11
references.context: Two men in white robes address disciples at the Ascension: 'Men of Galilee, why do you stand looking up toward heaven? This Jesus...will come again in the same way you saw him go.'

references.name: John 20:29
references.context: Jesus' words to Thomas: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'

references.name: Nicene Creed
references.context: Recitation of creedal language: 'On the third day, he rose again...He ascended into heaven and is seated at the right hand of the Father.'

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Episcopal priest, professor, and author cited as intellectual authority on the Ascension.

references.name: Gospel Medicine
references.context: Barbara Brown Taylor's book quoted: 'Ascension Day...is the day that 11 people with nothing but a promise and a prayer consented to become the church.'

references.name: The Picture of Dorian Gray (Oscar Wilde, implicit)
references.context: Hypothetical reference to 'Dorian Gray-esque existence' to describe if Jesus had remained physically present on earth.

references.name: Saint Patrick
references.context: Saint Patrick cited as author of a prayer encapsulating the theological magnitude of the Ascension.

references.name: Saint Patrick's Breastplate
references.context: Medieval Irish Christian prayer attributed to Saint Patrick, quoted extensively: 'Christ with me, Christ before me, Christ behind me...'

references.name: Pentecost (Acts 2)
references.context: Referenced as the 'great 50th day' following Ascension when the Holy Spirit is sent to disciples.

references.name: Trinity Sunday liturgical tradition
references.context: Saint Patrick's Breastplate mentioned as sung on Trinity Sunday (or in May); liturgical calendar reference.

primary_theme: The Ascension fundamentally transforms the nature of Christ's presence from spatially-temporally bounded to omnipresent and universal, making possible the Church's mission and the subsequent gift of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-scholarly reflection combining liturgical anchoring (feast day context), theological argument (spatio-temporal analysis), literary allusions, patristic quotation, and congregational invitation to deeper understanding of an underappreciated theological doctrine.

references.name: Howard Finster
references.context: Outsider artist who believed he was called by God through visions to spread the gospel through art; designed album covers for R.E.M. and Talking Heads; embodied the principle of universal love ('I never met someone I did not love').

references.name: R.E.M. and Talking Heads
references.context: Rock bands for whom Howard Finster designed album covers in the 1980s, enabling him to reach millions with gospel through commercial art.

references.name: Sister Macrina (Benedictine)
references.context: Quoted: 'I have never been one to limit God to the scriptures, though the scriptures have nourished me well. God is not imprisoned anywhere, not in the Bible, nor the tabernacle.'

references.name: Acts 10-11 (Peter's vision and the conversion of Cornelius)
references.context: The primary biblical text for the sermon; Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals and his visit to Cornelius' household, instructing him not to make distinctions between Jews and Gentiles.

references.name: Torah dietary laws
references.context: Invoked to explain Peter's shock at the command to eat unclean animals, which would defile an observant Jewish man according to deeply held religious beliefs.

references.name: Pentecost
references.context: Alluded to when describing the Holy Spirit falling on Cornelius' household as 'a little Pentecost' (reference to Acts 2).

references.name: Outsider art
references.context: An American art form created by untrained artists expressing religious themes and Bible stories through available materials, described as 'the stained glass of the common man.'

references.name: Mysticism / visionary experience
references.context: The sermon emphasizes visions (Peter's, Finster's, Macrina's theology) as means through which God communicates transformation and breaks human limitations.

primary_theme: God cannot be limited by human imagination or boundaries; Peter's vision teaches that true gospel proclamation requires meeting people where they are, breaking bread together, and recognizing the divine in all people without distinction.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling interwoven with pastoral reflection; the preacher uses the extended example of Howard Finster and his art collection to introduce theological concepts, then grounds these in scripture and thinker quotations, concluding with prophetic questions to the congregation about their own limitations on God.

references.name: Luke 13:31-35 (Pharisees warning Jesus about Herod; Jesus as mother hen; calling Herod a fox)
references.context: The primary gospel text for the sermon, depicting Pharisees warning Jesus that Herod wants to kill him, and Jesus' response about gathering Jerusalem's children like a hen gathering chicks.

references.name: John the Baptist (martyrdom under Herod)
references.context: Referenced as background to the threat against Jesus: 'Herod, who has already killed John the Baptist, wants to kill him.'

references.name: Psalms (unspecified)
references.context: The preacher mentions finding 'a few prayers and a psalm' to offer hope to a distressed parishioner about hardening hearts.

references.name: Pontius Pilate
references.context: Referenced via Professor Eric Smith's analysis: 'Herod was a puppet of Pontius Pilate and therefore of Rome.'

references.name: Ash Wednesday / Ashes to Go tradition
references.context: The sermon opens with a narrative about imposing ashes at Union Station on Ash Wednesday, establishing the Lenten context.

references.name: Lenten fasting tradition
references.context: The core theological concept throughout the sermon—fasting from things (traditionally food, social media, coffee) but specifically reframed here as 'fasting from hate' and 'fasting from jumping to conclusions.'

references.name: Biblical commentary tradition (disparagement of Pharisees in older scholarship)
references.context: The preacher critiques how 'the Pharisees are often disparaged in our thinking about Scripture and in older biblical commentary,' noting they are typically viewed as 'a foil for Jesus.'

references.name: Anti-Semitic sentiment and Lenten practice (historical church tradition)
references.context: The preacher discusses how 'Lent was a very dangerous time to be Jewish' and how 'Anti-Semitic sentiment and activity would always spike during Lent' due to readings that lumped together Jewish groups.

references.name: Eric Smith (Professor)
references.context: Cited as having 'led many dean's forums here at St. John's' and offering an interpretation that Herod (not the Pharisees) represents the non-Jewish ruling powers seeking Jesus' death.

references.name: Unnamed biblical commentator (on Pharisees as rigid hypocrites)
references.context: The preacher quotes a commentator who argues that 'customary interpretation has made all of the Pharisees into rigid hypocrites. Some probably were. Find me a crowd of 100 people...and I will find you at least 15 people in that crowd who are rigid hypocrites.'

references.name: Modern scholarly reassessment of Jesus and Pharisees
references.context: The preacher references 'scholars in more recent time' who argue 'Jesus himself was more closely aligned with the Pharisees than any other Jewish group in their observance of the law, Sabbath, tradition, in their curiosity, in their willingness to engage with Jesus in discussion and debate.'

references.name: Anti-Semitism is a light sleeper (unattributed quote)
references.context: The preacher cites this phrase from 'an interview this week' as encapsulating the persistent danger of Anti-Semitic sentiment during Lent.

references.name: Dualism / binary thinking (theological critique)
references.context: The sermon repeatedly critiques the tendency toward 'dualizing and characterizing' in both Scripture and life—the easy division into 'good, evil, in, out, love, hate, right, wrong.'

references.name: Interpretive complexity and textual close reading
references.context: The preacher advocates for careful, curious engagement with Scripture: 'if we read this closely, if we pay attention to the text,' and encourages 'looking under the proverbial hood' rather than accepting surface-level interpretations.

primary_theme: During Lent, Christians are called to fast not merely from food or comforts, but from hatred, reductive thinking, incuriosity, and the dangerous tendency to divide the world and Scripture into simplistic binaries of good and evil—a practice demonstrated through a fresh, sympathetic reading of the Pharisees in Luke's gospel.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with scholarly reinterpretation; the preacher weaves personal pastoral anecdotes (the distressed parishioner, the Ash Wednesday conversation) with biblical scholarship and gentle prophetic critique, using close textual reading and recontextualization to challenge conventional interpretations while maintaining an invitational, conversational tone.

references.name: Holden Caulfield
references.context: Preacher suggests young adults coming to Ash Wednesday might be like Holden Caulfield who 'have no use for phonies,' implying Ash Wednesday's honesty appeals to those seeking authenticity.

references.name: J.D. Salinger
references.context: Referenced through 'The Catcher in the Rye' character Holden Caulfield to illustrate the appeal of Ash Wednesday's counter-cultural honesty.

references.name: Book of Joel (Old Testament)
references.context: Joel 1-2 referenced throughout: plague of locusts, calling people to solemn assembly, gathering all people including children and infants; also Joel 2:13 quoted: 'Please God, rend my heart and not my clothing.'

references.name: Joel 2:13
references.context: Quoted directly: 'Please God, rend my heart and not my clothing' as the standard for genuine repentance over external ritual.

references.name: Funeral liturgy/Commendation of the Dead
references.context: Preacher cites the traditional funeral commendation: 'You only are immortal, the creator and maker of mankind, and we are mortal, formed of the earth, and to earth shall we return.'

references.name: Genesis 3:19 (implied)
references.context: The phrase 'You are dust, and to dust you shall return' is the foundational liturgical formula repeated throughout the sermon as the core truth of Ash Wednesday.

references.name: Ash Wednesday liturgy
references.context: The entire sermon reflects on and explores the liturgical practice of imposing ashes with the words 'Remember that you are dust, and to dust you shall return.'

references.name: Christian eschatology/mortality theology
references.context: The sermon engages deeply with Christian theology of death, human finitude, and the paradox that accepting mortality liberates one to live fully.

references.name: Lenten practices/tradition
references.context: Ash Wednesday marked as the beginning of the 40-day season of Lent understood as a season of repentance, self-examination, and preparation for Easter.

references.name: Palm Sunday (implicit reference)
references.context: The ashes imposed come from burned palms from the previous year's Palm Sunday, linking Christ's triumphal entry to human mortality.

references.name: Trinitarian theology
references.context: Reference to 'the triune God' as the only immortal being, contrasting human mortality with divine immortality.

references.name: Kingdom of God theology
references.context: Sermon concludes by calling congregation to 'participate in the inbreaking of God's kingdom here and now,' reflecting realized eschatology.

references.name: American consumer culture critique
references.context: Preacher critiques the multi-billion dollar anti-aging industry (moisturizing, medicating, exercising) as counter to Ash Wednesday's message of mortality.

primary_theme: Ash Wednesday offers counter-cultural honesty about human mortality and finitude that paradoxically frees people to live authentically and purposefully, while communal gathering amplifies this transformative realization.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic critique, blending personal narrative (children's misunderstanding of ashes), liturgical theology, cultural commentary, and call to communal action; uses questions to invite self-reflection and employs contrast between cultural messages and Christian truth.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Preacher contrasts Luke's 'Sermon on the Plain' with Matthew's 'Sermon on the Mount,' favoring Luke's version for its practical component.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Matthew's version of Jesus's teaching is mentioned as 'more Colorado appropriate' but less favored than Luke's telling.

references.name: Luke 6:20-26 (Beatitudes and Woes)
references.context: The core text of the sermon: Jesus teaching about blessings for the poor, hungry, and weeping, and woes for the rich, full, and laughing.

references.name: The Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55)
references.context: Mary's song is cited as expressing a world order turned upside down, with a direct through-line to the Sermon on the Plain; 'he has brought down the powerful from their thrones and lifted up the lowly.'

references.name: Luke 19:1-10 (Zacchaeus)
references.context: Referenced to show Jesus has a close relationship with a wealthy man, demonstrating Jesus does not despise the wealthy.

references.name: Luke 15:11-32 (Parable of the Prodigal Son)
references.context: Cited as an example where the father's wealth is not judged negatively and the father is typically interpreted as the hero.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer, Compline service
references.context: Quoted for its protective prayer language: 'protection for all who work and weep, who are weary, sick, or dying' and to 'shield the joyous.'

references.name: Liberation theology
references.context: Implicit in the sermon's emphasis on societal power structures, upending injustices, and critiquing wealth inequality as central to Jesus's message.

references.name: Pastoral theology/care
references.context: The preacher characterizes the woes as 'Christ's pastoral concerns for his flock' rather than curses, reflecting her role as canon pastor.

references.name: Dualistic interpretation (of blessings/woes)
references.context: The preacher identifies and critiques an 'all-too-easy and dualistic interpretation' that virtue lies in poverty and vice in wealth.

references.name: Realized eschatology
references.context: Implicit in the discussion that 'for Luke, the kingdom of God was not some far-off promise' and that Jesus's followers must 'do some of the work now.'

references.name: Unnamed scholarship on Jesus and wealth
references.context: The preacher references 'scholarship from people who are so much smarter than I' to support that Jesus does not despise the wealthy.

primary_theme: Jesus's Sermon on the Plain invites all disciples—especially the comfortable and privileged—to actively partner in God's kingdom-building work by recognizing and countering societal injustice, not through guilt or romanticizing poverty, but through awareness that present abundance can make us spiritually vulnerable and forgetful of God's reign.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic challenge: the preacher uses narrative analysis of scripture, personal testimony, liturgical reference, and gentle but direct address to the congregation's privilege to invite discomfort and reorientation toward justice work.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: The preacher reads Matthew's account of Jesus' birth, including details of Mary and Joseph's betrothal, the angel appearing to Joseph in a dream, and the story of the Magi.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The preacher reads Luke's telling of the need to go to Bethlehem, angels appearing to shepherds, and the heavenly host filling the sky.

references.name: Matthew 2:13-23 (Flight to Egypt and Herod's slaughter of the innocents)
references.context: The primary text for the sermon: Joseph is warned in a dream to flee to Egypt to escape Herod's decree to kill all male children under two; Joseph obeys and later returns to Galilee when Herod dies.

references.name: Exodus narrative (Moses and Pharaoh's infanticide)
references.context: The preacher draws parallels between Herod's slaughter of the innocents and Pharaoh's call for the death of Hebrew baby boys, noting the irony that Egypt—once a place of enslavement—becomes a place of refuge for Jesus.

references.name: Jewish interpretive tradition regarding dreams and prophecy
references.context: The preacher notes that Joseph had 'scriptural precedents' for heeding 'Oniric warnings,' suggesting a tradition of valuing divinely-given dreams as authoritative guidance.

references.name: Messianic prophecy fulfillment tradition
references.context: The preacher emphasizes that 'ancient prophecies come to life at every step of this journey' and that details in Matthew are 'essential to the fulfillment of prophecy and central to the life that Jesus was to live.'

references.name: Emmanuel (God with us)
references.context: The preacher references 'Emmanuel' as a theme of hope juxtaposed against the story of genocide and exile, invoking the messianic title.

references.name: Sentimentalism critique in religious narrative
references.context: The preacher states that Matthew's Christmas story 'keeps Christmas from being saccharine and full of sentimentality,' suggesting a theological critique of overly romanticized nativity accounts.

primary_theme: The harsh reality of violence, oppression, and human vulnerability embedded in the nativity story challenges sentimental Christmas piety and reveals that Jesus' incarnation was always intended to confront and overcome systems of power-driven evil.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with historical-typological exegesis: the preacher uses personal family tradition to establish intimacy, then develops a sustained parallel between the Exodus narrative and the Flight to Egypt, moving toward prophetic social commentary on contemporary violence against children.

references.name: Caroline Lewis
references.context: A former seminary professor and Johannine scholar cited as wondering whether theology of incarnation leans too heavily on the cross and Jesus' death, backgrounding Jesus' entire life.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: Referenced throughout as presenting incarnation as a cosmic event and as a correction to sentimentalism; used to support the argument that God's revelation is experienced through incarnation, crucifixion, resurrection, and ascension.

references.name: John 10:10
references.context: Quoted by Caroline Lewis: 'I came that they might have life and have it abundantly' to illustrate God's revelation of love and grace.

references.name: John 1 (Prologue)
references.context: Referenced as hearkening back to Genesis and presenting 'In the beginning was the word' followed by 'the light shines in the darkness and the darkness did not overcome it.'

references.name: Genesis 1
references.context: Alluded to as the background for John's prologue; 'In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth, and God's first creation is the light.'

references.name: Martha and Mary (Luke 10:38-42)
references.context: Referenced through narrative characters to encourage listeners to shift from 'doing all the things' (Martha) to sitting at Jesus' feet and listening (Mary).

references.name: Episcopal Church collect for Christmas
references.context: The sermon's collect is quoted: 'You have poured upon us the new light of your incarnate word. Grant that this light, enkindled in our hearts, may shine forth in our lives through Jesus Christ our Lord.'

references.name: Johannine theology / Johannine scholarship
references.context: The sermon engages with scholarly interpretation of the Fourth Gospel, particularly how John presents incarnation as cosmic and corrective to narrow atonement theology.

references.name: Atonement theology / Substitutionary atonement
references.context: Implicitly critiqued through discussion of the phrase 'Jesus died for you' and the guilt and control mechanisms it creates; the sermon argues for a broader incarnational theology.

references.name: Incarnational theology
references.context: The primary intellectual framework: the sermon advocates for a 'fulsome theology of incarnation' that extends beyond the cross to encompass the entire arc of Jesus' life.

references.name: Emmanuel (God with us)
references.context: Referenced at the end: 'Jesus, incarnate God, Emmanuel, God with us' as the culmination of incarnational theology.

primary_theme: The sermon argues for a broader incarnational theology that emphasizes what Jesus lived for—God becoming human so we might become like God—rather than narrowly focusing on the salvific necessity of Jesus' death.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-scholarly argument combining personal pastoral reflection with scholarly theological citation (Caroline Lewis), prophetic challenge to inherited guilt-based theology, and meditative invitation to contemplate the cosmic meaning of incarnation.

references.name: The Lectionary (Three-Year Cycle)
references.context: Pearson uses the lectionary's three-year cycle as the framework for her reflection on encountering the same gospel readings multiple times across years.

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: The sermon focuses on Luke's Advent readings, specifically Year C of the lectionary cycle, and contrasts expected nativity passages with apocalyptic end-times material.

references.name: Lamentations 3:22-23 (implied: 'mercies new every morning')
references.context: Pearson states 'Scripture is often like mercy, new every morning,' drawing on the Lamentations passage about God's mercies being new each morning.

references.name: United Church of Christ Tagline: 'God is Still Speaking'
references.context: Pearson cites this UCC tagline from a decade ago (seen on bumper stickers and church marquees) as expressing a theological conviction that God continues to speak dynamically through multiple channels.

references.name: Luke 21:25-28 (or similar apocalyptic Advent readings)
references.context: The sermon references apocalyptic readings about 'the Son of Man coming in a cloud with power and glory' and the call to 'stand up and raise our heads' when redemption draws near.

references.name: Luke 1:26-38 (Annunciation to Mary)
references.context: Pearson notes that congregants might have expected the angel's visit announcing Mary as God-bearer on the First Sunday of Advent, but instead find apocalyptic material.

references.name: Luke 1:39-45 (Visitation/Meeting of Mary and Elizabeth)
references.context: Pearson mentions the expected reading of Mary and Elizabeth meeting with their unborn babies leaping in recognition, but this is displaced by apocalyptic content.

references.name: Evening Prayer (Anglican/Episcopal liturgy)
references.context: Pearson references a line in Evening Prayer about 'shielding the joyous' and suggests it needs a footnote for the month of December.

references.name: Advent Tradition
references.context: The sermon uses Advent as a liturgical and spiritual framework calling Christians to attentiveness, listening, watching, and waiting in anticipation of Christ's coming.

references.name: Apocalyptic Literature/Eschatology
references.context: Pearson discusses 'apocalyptic readings' and 'scary images of end times' that have been part of recent Sunday lessons, setting the stage for Advent.

references.name: Mathematical Word Problems (pedagogical reference)
references.context: Pearson opens with a self-deprecating reference to math teachers and 'the church version of if the two trains leave the station at the same time' word problems.

references.name: The Episcopal Church
references.context: Pearson identifies herself as part of the Episcopal tradition and wishes the church had adopted the UCC's 'God is still speaking' tagline.

primary_theme: Advent calls Christians to attentive listening and watchfulness for God's ongoing, dynamic speaking through multiple channels, amid the distractions and busyness of the December season.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with conversational accessibility; combines personal narrative (repeated preaching on the same texts), gentle humor (lottery ticket comment), liturgical/scriptural reasoning, and prophetic exhortation to counter cultural distraction with spiritual attentiveness.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Today's gospel lesson from Mark is presented as a two-act drama featuring the widow's mite story.

references.name: Mark 12:41-44 (Widow's Mite)
references.context: The second half of the text is familiar to many churchgoers; the widow places two small coins in the treasury.

references.name: Mark 12:38-40 (Jesus criticizes scribes)
references.context: Immediately before the widow appears, Jesus castigates the scribes for their pretense, arrogance, and for devouring widows' houses.

references.name: Hebrew Scriptures (on care for widows)
references.context: A long and important tradition written throughout Hebrew scriptures that widows are to be cared for from the temple treasury.

references.name: Parables in the Gospels
references.context: This story bears such resemblance to so many of the parables in the Gospels in being completely open-ended with holes for theological imagination.

references.name: Temple table-turning (Matthew 21:12-13, Mark 11:15-17, Luke 19:45-46)
references.context: Jesus has turned over the tables in the temple just prior to today's text, indicating his displeasure with temple leaders.

references.name: Jesus's self-sacrifice
references.context: The widow's giving of her whole life is described as perhaps a foreshadowing of Jesus soon to give his whole life.

references.name: Preachers in Sneakers (Instagram account)
references.context: Used as a contemporary parallel to scribes' long robes indicating elite class status; evangelical preachers wearing expensive sneakers as modern performative display.

references.name: Episcopal tradition
references.context: Preacher humorously notes that as Episcopalians directing this mental movie, the widow would anachronistically cross herself reverentially.

references.name: Stewardship tradition
references.context: The widow's mite is a favorite of many preachers during stewardship season, used to encourage sacrificial giving.

references.name: Liberation or prophetic theology (implicit)
references.context: The preacher's concern about how religious institutions marginalize the poor and the call to examine institutional structures recalls liberation theological critique.

primary_theme: A reinterpretation of the widow's mite story that critiques exploitative religious institutions while also affirming the widow's faithfulness, ultimately calling for communities of humility, just leadership, and genuine care for the vulnerable.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection and prophetic challenge; the preacher uses vivid imaginative visualization of the widow's story, then destabilizes the traditional reading through contextual reframing and raises critical questions about institutional ethics and the abuse of the poor.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: The Bartimaeus healing story appears in Mark 10:46-52, described as 'the final healing story in the Gospel of Mark' as Jesus heads toward Jerusalem.

references.name: Mark 10:46-52 (Bartimaeus healing)
references.context: The primary biblical text analyzed throughout the sermon; a healing narrative featuring a blind beggar named Bartimaeus outside Jericho.

references.name: Synoptic Gospels
references.context: The Bartimaeus story 'appears in all three synoptic gospels,' indicating it held significant importance across Matthew, Mark, and Luke.

references.name: Gospel healing stories (30 or so in the gospels)
references.context: The Bartimaeus narrative is noted as one of only a couple of healing stories out of approximately 30 in the gospels where the person healed is named.

references.name: Mark 10:35-45 (James and John/Sons of Zebedee)
references.context: Jesus asks James and John the same question he asks Bartimaeus ('What do you want me to do for you?'), but they seek prestige while Bartimaeus seeks mercy.

references.name: Previous healing of a blind man in Mark
references.context: The Bartimaeus story is described as 'a bookend to Jesus' other healing of the blind man just a few chapters ago' (likely Mark 8:22-26).

references.name: Messiah/Son of David theology
references.context: Bartimaeus' proclamation of Jesus as 'Son of David' is identified as 'a title that Jesus has not used yet during His public ministry' and represents messianic recognition.

references.name: Lectionary tradition
references.context: The sermon references 'last week's lectionary' and the Twenty-third Sunday after Pentecost liturgical calendar, situating the text within liturgical practice.

references.name: Episcopal/Moravian Church tradition
references.context: Preacher mentions a preschool at 'an Episcopal Moravian Church in Charlotte, North Carolina,' establishing denominational context.

primary_theme: The Bartimaeus healing story reveals multiple layers of blindness and sight: physical, social, and spiritual—where Jesus heals not only the blind beggar's vision but also the crowd's spiritual blindness, enabling them to see his full humanity and welcome him into community.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection with theological depth; the preacher uses a personal anecdote (her son naming the blind man) as an entry point, then employs close textual analysis and hermeneutical layering to explore multiple meanings of blindness/sight, concluding with practical pastoral application about asking and listening in community.

references.name: Sam Portaro
references.context: Episcopal priest cited as intellectual authority on angels and imagination; contends that literalism in faith prevents engagement with theological imagination; invites readers to dream and imagine unseen realms.

references.name: Genesis 28:12-15 (Jacob's Ladder)
references.context: Old Testament reading featuring Jacob's dream of a ladder reaching to heaven with angels ascending and descending; God speaks to Jacob about the covenant with Abraham and promises of land and offspring.

references.name: Abraham
references.context: Referenced as recipient of God's covenant promise regarding land and numerous offspring; angels deliver messages to Abraham throughout Scripture.

references.name: Moses
references.context: Listed as biblical figure who received angel messages from God.

references.name: Joseph and Mary
references.context: Listed as recipients of angel messages announcing Jesus's birth.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer (BCP)
references.context: Referenced as liturgical text containing language about joining voices with angels and archangels in worship and praise.

references.name: Saint Michael
references.context: Named archangel celebrated on this feast day; described as powerful agent of God who wards off evil, delivers peace at end of life, and serves as patron saint of police, paramedics, warriors, and the military; guardian prince of Israel.

references.name: Gabriel
references.context: Named as one of four archangels specifically called by name in the Bible.

references.name: Uriel
references.context: Named as one of four archangels specifically called by name in the Bible.

references.name: Raphael
references.context: Named as one of four archangels specifically called by name in the Bible.

references.name: Christian artistic tradition
references.context: Angels depicted in Christian art with human form, wings (signifying swiftness), swords (signifying power), and dazzling garments (signifying enlightenment).

references.name: Mystical/Imaginative Theology
references.context: Implicit tradition opposing literalism; emphasizes imagination, dreaming, and theological flight as worthy spiritual pursuits rather than dismissible mythology.

references.name: Guardian angel tradition
references.context: Time-honored folk practice at Swan (a campus in Tennessee) where students, alumni, and townsfolk touch car ceilings at gates to pick up protective guardian angels for travel away from campus.

references.name: Covenant theology
references.context: Biblical framework used to explain God's promises to Abraham regarding land and offspring, connected to Jacob's dream experience.

primary_theme: Angels function as invitations to theological imagination and spiritual openness, moving believers beyond literalism to encounter the divine in both visible and invisible realms.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining personal narrative (guardian angel tradition at Swan), scholarly biblical interpretation (covenant theology), theological authority (Sam Portaro), liturgical grounding (BCP), and imaginative invitation to spiritual practice.

references.name: Book of James
references.context: The epistle is the primary text for the sermon, discussing James's metaphors about the tongue, rudders, and fresh/brackish water to address how words can bless or curse.

references.name: Mark's Gospel
references.context: Jesus teaches that 'there is nothing outside of a person that by going in can defile. But the things that come out are what defile,' connecting internal thoughts to external speech.

references.name: Washington Irving
references.context: Quoted for the observation: 'the tongue is the only tool that gets sharper with use,' emphasizing how words become more damaging over time.

references.name: Book of Proverbs
references.context: Referenced as parallel wisdom literature to James, filled with 'moral exhortations and precepts,' establishing James's affinity to Jewish wisdom tradition.

references.name: Jewish Wisdom Literature
references.context: The preacher notes that 'Many consider James to have an affinity to Jewish wisdom literature,' situating the epistle within a broader intellectual tradition.

references.name: Baptismal Covenant
references.context: Referenced for its language about 'when we fall into sin' (not 'if'), emphasizing the assumption that humans will make mistakes as part of the human condition.

references.name: Austin Powers
references.context: Cited as pop culture colloquialization of the wisdom 'Do you kiss your mother with that mouth?' demonstrating how the principle about words flows through modern media.

references.name: SpongeBob SquarePants
references.context: Referenced alongside other pop culture examples that colloquialize the wisdom about blessing and cursing from the same mouth.

references.name: The Avengers
references.context: Cited as another pop culture source using the 'Do you kiss your mother with that mouth?' line about the duality of words.

references.name: Wayne's World
references.context: Referenced as a film source of the catchphrase 'Do you kiss your mother with that mouth?' illustrating how wisdom permeates popular entertainment.

references.name: The Gospel of Mark
references.context: Contains the teaching that evil thoughts and feelings begin within hearts and minds, and what comes out of the mouth defiles a person.

references.name: Imago Dei (Image of God)
references.context: Invoked when preacher says we should 'speak love to one another' because our neighbors are 'made in the likeness of God,' grounding ethical speech in theological anthropology.

primary_theme: Human accountability for speech: the tongue's power to bless or curse requires awareness, humility, and intention to speak words of love and blessing rather than harm.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining narrative storytelling (summer chapel experience, personal anecdotes about a two-day drive), biblical exegesis (close reading of James and Mark), pop culture references for contemporary relevance, and prophetic challenge about divisive rhetoric in modern culture, culminating in practical spiritual guidance for the congregation.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Luke is identified as the synoptic gospel most saturated with stories of Jesus praying, compared to Matthew and Mark.

references.name: Luke 3:21-22 (Jesus' baptism and prayer)
references.context: Referenced as the first of multiple instances where prayer precedes dramatic spiritual events in Luke's gospel.

references.name: Luke 9:28-36 (Transfiguration account)
references.context: The primary text being interpreted: Jesus prays on a mountain, is transfigured, and God speaks to the disciples.

references.name: Selection of the twelve apostles (Luke 6:12-13)
references.context: Cited as an instance where Jesus' selection of the twelve followed an entire night of prayer.

references.name: Peter's confession of Jesus as Messiah (Luke 9:18-20)
references.context: Referenced as occurring in the context of Jesus praying.

references.name: Jesus praying in Gethsemane (Luke 22:41-44)
references.context: Listed as one of the critical moments where Jesus prays when he needs guidance, peace, and to reconnect with God.

references.name: Jesus praying from the cross (Luke 23:34, 23:46)
references.context: Cited as an example of Jesus praying at a critical moment of distress.

references.name: The Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Referenced multiple times as the source of liturgical prayers and the catechism definition of prayer used in Episcopal tradition.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer Catechism - Definition of Prayer
references.context: Quoted directly: 'Prayer is responding to God by thought and by deeds, with or without words.' This definition becomes the theological linchpin of the sermon.

references.name: The three synoptic gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke comparison)
references.context: Luke is distinguished among the three synoptics as most saturated with prayer narratives.

references.name: Apocalyptic imagery and biblical mysticism
references.context: The sermon references the mysterious and fantastical elements of the transfiguration narrative: dazzling light, heroes from Israel's history, cloud, heavenly voice.

references.name: Episcopal theological practice and prayer anxiety
references.context: The preacher notes that 'Episcopalians are notoriously insecure about our ability to pray in our own words,' suggesting a cultural-theological anxiety within the tradition.

references.name: Israel's history and prophetic tradition
references.context: Referenced implicitly through mention of 'heroes from Israel's history' appearing in the transfiguration (likely Moses and Elijah).

primary_theme: Prayer as responsive listening and transformation: the transfiguration reveals that prayer changes us when we understand it not merely as speaking to God with words, but as a complete response—through thought, deeds, and silence—that opens us to God's guidance and transformation.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with scholarly grounding, combining exegetical observation (comparative synoptic analysis, textual references) with personal testimony and congregational self-recognition (Episcopalian prayer anxiety), moving toward prophetic invitation (prayer as accessible spiritual practice).

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: The entire sermon is a homiletical exposition of Mark 6:1-13, exploring the theme that Jesus' identity is often unrecognized even by those closest to him.

references.name: Mark 4:35-41 (Jesus Calms the Storm)
references.context: Referenced as a recent precedent where disciples witness a miracle but still ask 'Who then is this that even the wind and the sea obey him?'

references.name: Mark 5:21-43 (Jairus and the Hemorrhaging Woman)
references.context: Cited as example of those without prior reason to believe recognizing Jesus' healing power, contrasting with hometown rejection.

references.name: Mark 3:20-30 (Jesus Accused of Beelzebul)
references.context: Referenced as earlier instance of Jesus' family attempting to 'rein him in' and accusations of collusion with demons.

references.name: Great Commission (Matthew 28:19-20 / Mark 6:7-13 variant)
references.context: Identified as 'Mark's version of the Great Commission' where Jesus sends disciples to 'Go...Perform it...Share the message of repentance.'

references.name: Catechumenate
references.context: The preacher references hearing from catechumenate participants who feel 'sheepish for not knowing much about Jesus or Scripture or the church.'

references.name: Peter (Apostle)
references.context: Used as a narrative and relatable character example; preacher humorously imagines Peter's pairing challenges and expresses personal identification with him.

references.name: The Twelve Disciples
references.context: Central to the second half of the sermon, showing how Jesus empowers and sends them forth in pairs to replicate his ministry.

references.name: Hospitality / Stranger Kindness
references.context: Emphasized as the disciples 'relied on the kindness and hospitality of strangers,' invoking a biblical/Christian virtue tradition.

references.name: Community and Accompaniment Theology
references.context: The preacher articulates that disciples were sent 'two by two' to affirm that 'all need accompaniment for accountability, safety, support, verification of witness, and companionship,' rooted in Christian relational theology.

primary_theme: Jesus' identity remains unrecognized even by those closest to him, but his empowerment of disciples sent in pairs demonstrates that faith community and accompaniment are essential for witnessing and proclaiming the gospel.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative exposition; the preacher uses biblical storytelling, personal testimony about experiencing God in community, gentle humor (particularly about Peter), and direct pastoral address to the congregation's existential struggles with belonging and faith knowledge.

references.name: Book of Job
references.context: The entire sermon is built around the Old Testament book of Job, particularly God's response to Job's suffering and questioning.

references.name: Job 38 (divine speeches)
references.context: God asks Job, 'Were you there when I laid the foundation of the earth?' demonstrating the incommensurability between human and divine knowledge.

references.name: Ellen Davis
references.context: Old Testament theologian from Duke Divinity School quoted extensively on Job's transformation and the meaning of having more children as renewal of mind rather than divine compensation.

references.name: Biblical scholars (unnamed)
references.context: Preacher notes 'there are as many interpretations of the book of Job as there are biblical scholars' and references reading commentators on Job this week.

references.name: Anonymous commentator on chaos monsters
references.context: A commentator noted that God may be subtly pointing out that humans are 'chaos monsters' too—unpredictable and capable of generating both life and destruction.

references.name: Job's friends (Eliphaz, Bildad, Zophar)
