references.context: Described as 'unhelpful agents of a thought experiment' who propose that Job's suffering results from his own sin or faithlessness.

references.name: Satan/the Accuser
references.context: Referenced as a participant in the heavenly court scene at the opening of Job, where the name 'means the accuser.'

references.name: Behemoth and Leviathan
references.context: Mythical and dangerous creatures from Job's divine speeches, used to illustrate God's creation of both order and chaos.

references.name: Pentecost liturgical tradition
references.context: Sermon title designates it as 'The Fifth Sunday after Pentecost,' placing it within the Christian liturgical calendar.

references.name: Folk tale genre
references.context: Preacher notes that Job blends together folk tale, hymn, individual lament, prophetic oracle, and didactic poem—establishing genre literacy.

references.name: Prophetic oracle genre
references.context: One of several literary genres blended in Job's composition, referenced to show the text's theological complexity.

references.name: Didactic poem tradition
references.context: Identified as one of the genres woven into Job's structure, suggesting the text's instructional theological purpose.

references.name: Divine speech/theophany tradition
references.context: The 'divine speeches' are identified as official theological naming by scholars; God appears in a whirlwind, linking to Old Testament theophany tradition.

references.name: Job 40-41 (behemoth and Leviathan descriptions)
references.context: Detailed descriptions of mythical creatures presented as part of God's self-revelation through the cosmic order.

references.name: Job's final restoration (Job 42:10-17)
references.context: Ellen Davis interprets Job's willingness to have seven more sons and three daughters as evidence of psychological and spiritual transformation, not divine compensation.

references.name: Theodicy question (implicit)
references.context: The sermon engages with the classical theodicy problem—'If God is good and powerful, why is there suffering?'—without resolving it.

references.name: Process theology (implicit)
references.context: Preacher's assertion that the world is 'not designed to prevent suffering' and that 'human freedom can generate abundant life or terrible destruction' suggests openness to process theological perspectives.

primary_theme: God's response to human suffering is not to explain or justify it, but to invite humans into a deeper understanding of their place in a complex, beautiful, and unpredictable cosmos—and to trust not in answers but in God's presence and delight in creation.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection and scholarly analysis; the preacher opens with a personal family anecdote, builds through literary and theological analysis of Job's genres and structure, and closes with a reflection on human vulnerability and trust.

references.name: Genesis 1
references.context: The preacher cites Genesis 1 to introduce the Hebrew word 'Ruach' (breath/spirit/wind) as it appears when God's wind sweeps over the waters at creation.

references.name: Genesis 2
references.context: Referenced as a continuation of the creation narrative where Ruach appears, though the specific passage is not completed in the transcript.

references.name: Psalm (appointed for Pentecost Sunday)
references.context: The preacher notes that the appointed psalm reminds us that God can take breath away and death results in return to dust.

references.name: Ezekiel 37 (Valley of Dry Bones vision)
references.context: Extended central passage: God shows Ezekiel a vision of dry bones being revived with breath to give hope to exiled Israelites; the preacher quotes 'O dry bones, hear the word of the Lord' and 'I will cause breath, ruach, to enter you and you shall live.'

references.name: Acts (Pentecost account)
references.context: The preacher mentions that disciples witnessed the gift of the Spirit in Acts, connecting it to Ezekiel's vision and John's gospel.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: Referenced as John's testimony to the gift of the Spirit, though no specific passage is cited.

references.name: C.S. Lewis
references.context: Lewis's Aslan from The Chronicles of Narnia is used as a literary parallel: 'just as C.S. Lewis's Aslan breathes on and reanimates the creatures who have been turned to stone by the White Witch.'

references.name: The Chronicles of Narnia (specifically 'The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe' or related Narnia work)
references.context: The scene of Aslan reanimating stone creatures is used as a contemporary literary analogy for God's breath bringing dry bones to life.

references.name: Spiritual song 'Dem Bones'
references.context: The preacher alludes to the spiritual 'Dem Bones' with its chorus about hearing the word of the Lord and bones reconnecting in 'a musical anatomy lesson.'

primary_theme: God's animating breath (Ruach) brings life and hope to spiritually dead places, transforming dry bones into new life—a promise fulfilled through the Holy Spirit at Pentecost.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling grounded in personal memoir (childhood sea monkeys) that functions as an extended metaphor for spiritual transformation, supported by scriptural exegesis of Ezekiel and reinforced with literary parallels and pastoral reflection on human experience of despair and restoration.

references.name: Psalm 133
references.context: The entire sermon is structured around Psalm 133, particularly the opening words 'Ecce quam bonum' (Behold, how good), which serve as the preacher's license plate motto and Sewanee's university seal.

references.name: John (Gospel account of Mary and the beloved disciple at the cross)
references.context: Referenced when discussing how Jesus created a new family from the cross for his mother and the beloved disciple, evoking community bonds.

references.name: Maundy Thursday tradition
references.context: Invoked when discussing ancient Near Eastern hospitality customs of anointing guests with oil upon entering a home.

references.name: Mary's anointing of Jesus with nard
references.context: Referenced as an example of extravagant, fragrant anointing days before Jesus's death, connecting to themes of abundance and hospitality.

references.name: Aaron (biblical figure)
references.context: Mentioned in connection with Psalm 133's imagery of oil running down Aaron's face into his beard and soaking his robe collar.

references.name: Sewanee (University of the South)
references.context: Identified as the preacher's alma mater whose university seal bears the motto 'Ecce quam bonum' as a touchstone for community.

references.name: Liturgical theology / liturgics tradition
references.context: The preacher references her seminary liturgics professor's insistence on using oil abundantly at baptisms, allowing them to be messy and authentic.

references.name: Episcopal Church tradition
references.context: Referenced through catechumen instruction and the statistic that 70% of Episcopalians come from another tradition, emphasizing welcome and unity.

references.name: Easter theology / Resurrection promises
references.context: Used to conclude the sermon with affirmation that in unity, 'the Lord has ordained the blessing, life forevermore' and that 'we will dwell in unity forever in Christ.'

references.name: Baptismal theology
references.context: The sermon's extended narrative about baptism practices serves as theological exposition on welcoming into community and the sacrament's role in creating unity.

references.name: Chrism (sacramental oil)
references.context: Referenced as the oil with which the sign of the cross is marked on heads during baptism, connecting sacramental action to community identity.

primary_theme: Christian community is built on unity (not uniformity) grounded in shared faith, grace, and authentic love, as exemplified in Psalm 133 and enacted through sacramental practices like baptism.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection. The preacher uses an extended personal anecdote (the pandemic baptism mishap) as the centerpiece to illustrate theological points about abundance, messiness, joy, and authentic community. This is combined with careful exegesis of Psalm 133, liturgical theology, and inviting challenges to the congregation.

references.name: John Bell
references.context: Bell's book 'Living the Psalms' influenced the preacher's thinking about Psalms as stories rich in meaning and applicable to lived existence.

references.name: Living the Psalms
references.context: The textbook used in the Lenten class on Psalms that shaped the preacher's interpretation of Psalms as personal prayers and stories.

references.name: Genesis 17 (Abraham and Sarah narrative)
references.context: Old Testament reading featuring Abraham at 99 years old receiving the covenant promise of a son, with language of abundance, blessing, and numerous offspring.

references.name: Psalm 22
references.context: The central psalm discussed throughout the sermon, identified as a lament psalm that begins with Jesus's words from the cross and moves from lament to praise.

references.name: Matthew 27:46 / Mark 15:34 (Jesus on the cross)
references.context: Jesus quoted Psalm 22's opening words ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?') from the cross during his suffering and sacrifice.

references.name: Gospel lesson (Mark 8:31 or parallel)
references.context: Jesus teaches disciples that the Son of Man must undergo suffering, be rejected, be killed, and rise after three days.

references.name: Lament tradition in Hebrew Scripture
references.context: The preacher notes that nearly half of the Psalms are laments, establishing this as a legitimate biblical tradition for expressing pain and despair.

references.name: Toxic positivity
references.context: The preacher critiques the contemporary cultural concept that 'good vibes only' and complaining is forbidden, contrasting it with the biblical permission to lament.

references.name: Lectionary structure
references.context: The preacher explains that the lectionary is constructed so that Psalms often respond to Old Testament readings, showing structural theological intentionality.

references.name: Episcopal Church tradition
references.context: Referenced in the context of trivia questions about Episcopal-related topics and the vestry retreat context.

references.name: Biblical commentary tradition
references.context: The preacher references 'one commentator' who cautions against viewing Psalm 22's sweep in a compact time frame, warning against overly neat resolutions.

primary_theme: Psalm 22 models a spiritually authentic path from lament through despair to praise, showing that God welcomes our deepest cries and that ultimate hope rests in God's never-ending promises—a message Jesus himself claimed from the cross.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining personal narrative (retreat trivia story), scholarly biblical interpretation (lectionary structure, psalm analysis), and theological counsel (permission for lament, critique of toxic positivity).

references.name: Ruth
references.context: Named as a character from Hebrew scriptures whom the preacher admires for her trickster qualities and use of guile to subvert power.

references.name: Esther
references.context: Named alongside Ruth as a Hebrew scripture character who employs guile to subvert power, whether embodied by individuals or systems.

references.name: Hebrew scriptures / Hebrew prophets
references.context: Referenced throughout as the foundation for understanding narrative technique, moral teaching, and Jesus's own intellectual formation.

references.name: Jesus's parables
references.context: Cited as examples of inverted or backward logic that delight the preacher and connect to Hebrew scriptural tradition.

references.name: Book of Jonah
references.context: The primary scriptural text analyzed; described by scholarly consensus as a novella with both narrative and didactic purpose.

references.name: Aesop / Aesop's fables
references.context: Used as a literary comparison: 'The story of Jonah is a moral tale, much like Aesop, and is designed to teach the audience something about themselves.'

references.name: Nineveh / Assyrian Empire
references.context: Historical context: Nineveh would become the capital of the Assyrian Empire, representing wickedness, bloodthirstiness, and imperial cruelty to the original audience.

references.name: The Princess Bride
references.context: Quoted ('It was inconceivable') to express Jonah's reaction to God's call to go to Nineveh.

references.name: Aldous Huxley
references.context: Named author of the poem 'Jonah' which describes Jonah's time in the fish's belly with poetic language about prayer and divine goodness.

references.name: Jonah (poem by Aldous Huxley)
references.context: Quoted passage: 'Seated upon the convex mound of one vast kidney, / Jonah prays and sings his canticles and hymns, / making the hollow vault resound God's goodness in mysterious ways, till the great fish spouts music.'

references.name: Jonah 1:1-3 (implied)
references.context: Paraphrased: 'Now the word of the Lord came to Jonah, and the Lord called him to arise and go to the great city of Nineveh to cry out against their evil. Jonah does indeed arise, but he runs away from God.'

references.name: Gospel reading (lectionary text, unnamed specific gospel)
references.context: Referenced as containing disciples who 'leave what [they are] doing and immediately follow God's call,' contrasted with Jonah's reluctance.

references.name: Jonah 3:3-10 (implied)
references.context: The core narrative: Jonah's reluctant preaching in Nineveh, the Ninevites' repentance, the king's sackcloth and fasting, and animals in sackcloth.

references.name: Scholarly consensus on Jonah as novella
references.context: Cited as contemporary scholarly understanding: Jonah is a novella that 'emphasizes both its narrative nature and its didactic purpose.'

references.name: Farcical behavior / farcical tradition in Hebrew scripture
references.context: Named as 'a common trope of Hebrew scripture' exemplified by the foreign ruler in Jonah.

references.name: Unnamed biblical commentator
references.context: Cited for the assertion: 'a little bit of Jonah lurks in every human heart, whimpering his insidious message of smug prejudice, empty traditionalism, and exclusive solidarity.'

references.name: Divine grace / divine mercy
references.context: Central theological concept: God's nature as gracious and merciful, extended universally and not contingent on human judgment of worthiness.

references.name: Redemption as gift and grace
references.context: Theological claim: 'Redemption is pure gift and grace' that humans do not own or control.

primary_theme: God's grace and mercy are universal and belong to God alone; humans must not presume to judge who deserves forgiveness or redemption, as exemplified in Jonah's resentment of Nineveh's salvation.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with scholarly grounding, pastoral reflection, literary analysis, and prophetic challenge—the preacher reconstructs the story's historical context and literary artistry before drawing contemporary ethical implications about prejudice and divine grace.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Preacher notes that in Matthew, Mark, and Luke, John the Baptist is presented, contrasting with the Gospel of John.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Listed alongside Matthew and Luke as gospels where John the Baptist appears as a baptizer of Jesus.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Listed alongside Matthew and Mark as gospels where John the Baptist appears as a baptizer of Jesus.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: The primary gospel for this sermon; preacher notes that in John's gospel, Jesus is not baptized by John, and Jesus' ministry is propelled by Mary at Cana rather than baptism.

references.name: John 1:6-8 (implied)
references.context: Quoted directly: 'There was a man sent from God whose name was John. He came as a witness to testify to the light so that all might believe through him.'

references.name: Wedding at Cana
references.context: Referenced as the event in John's gospel where Mary propels Jesus into ministry, not his baptism.

references.name: Magnificat
references.context: Preacher notes it was heard on this Sunday in Advent and mentions it in relation to Mary's role.

references.name: John's 'I am' statements
references.context: Preacher describes John's gospel as 'the I am gospel' where Jesus claims identity through statements like 'the vine,' 'the bread,' 'the good shepherd,' 'the great I am.'

references.name: Elijah
references.context: Referenced as one of the identities John the Baptist denies being asked about: 'Who are you? I am not Elijah.'

references.name: The Prophet
references.context: Referenced as one of the identities John the Baptist denies: 'Who are you? I am not the prophet.'

references.name: The Messiah
references.context: Referenced as the first identity John denies: 'Who are you? I am not the Messiah.'

references.name: Madeleine L'Engle
references.context: Preacher expresses 'deep love and admiration for the theological poetry and musings of Madeleine L'Engle' and cites her work.

references.name: Walking on Water
references.context: Madeleine L'Engle's book from which the closing quote is taken: 'We do not draw people to Christ by loudly discrediting what they believe...'

references.name: Gaudete Sunday
references.context: Named as the Third Sunday of Advent, with 'Gaudete' meaning 'rejoice,' identifying a liturgical tradition.

references.name: Advent liturgical tradition
references.context: The sermon is situated within the Third Sunday of Advent, with reference to rose candles and pink vestments as liturgical markers.

references.name: Episcopal theology and practice
references.context: Preacher addresses 'fellow Episcopalians' and notes that Episcopalians are 'not...any of the many denominations that use the word testimony with any degree of comfort.'

references.name: Witness theology
references.context: The sermon's central theological framework emphasizing witness and testimony, noting these appear 'over 50 times' in John's gospel and are foundational to faith.

references.name: Baptismal calling
references.context: Preacher invokes 'the calling of each and every one of us by virtue of our baptism to point to Jesus.'

primary_theme: Christians are called to be witnesses to Jesus as the light of the world, identifying themselves not by their own authority or identity but by their relationship to Christ and their role in pointing others toward Him.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with biblical theological exegesis; the preacher uses personal anecdote (Stockholm experience) to establish relatable metaphor, moves into careful scriptural analysis of John's gospel, and concludes with practical spiritual application and literary citation to inspire action.

references.name: David Brooks
references.context: Duke Divinity professor and writer Kate Bowler interviewed David Brooks about his latest book on her podcast; Brooks is cited as an authority on the epidemic of loneliness and social blindness in America.

references.name: How to Know a Person: The Art of Seeing Others Deeply and Being Deeply Seen
references.context: Brooks' book is cited as addressing the loss of the art of knowing one another and positing that many have forgotten how to be together.

references.name: Kate Bowler
references.context: Duke Divinity professor and podcast host who interviewed David Brooks about his book on knowing others.

references.name: U.S. Surgeon General
references.context: Cited as the authority declaring an epidemic of loneliness in America, supporting Brooks' thesis.

references.name: The Beatitudes
references.context: Matthew's Beatitudes are described as 'a veritable catalog of the ways we are seen and known by Jesus,' listing various conditions of blessing (mourning, meekness, hunger for justice, etc.).

references.name: Matthew 5 (Beatitudes passage)
references.context: The sermon references the Beatitudes from Matthew as the biblical foundation for understanding how Jesus sees and blesses different human conditions.

references.name: Kingdom of Heaven
references.context: Referenced as one of the blessings promised to those who are seen and known by Jesus.

references.name: The great cloud of witnesses
references.context: Hebrews 12:1 allusion; cited to describe the saints and multitudes gone before us who have been seen, known, blessed, and loved by God, connecting present congregation to the communion of saints.

references.name: Hebrews 12:1
references.context: The phrase 'great cloud of witnesses' is drawn from this passage, emphasizing the connection to All Saints and the continuity of faith across generations.

references.name: The Triune God
references.context: Referenced as the theological framework through which past, present, and future saints are connected through faith in the Trinity.

references.name: Baptismal vows and theology
references.context: The sermon uses the liturgical practice of baptism and baptismal promises as the concrete expression of being known and welcomed into the Christian community.

references.name: Eucharistic theology
references.context: Reference to Holy Communion and priests distributing the wafer as a tactile, embodied way of knowing and blessing members of the community.

references.name: All Saints Day theology
references.context: The sermon's occasion and framework: understanding the communion of saints and the ways living believers are connected to those who have gone before.

references.name: Genesis 37-50 (Joseph and his brothers)
references.context: The preacher invokes Joseph's forgiveness of his brothers as an exemplar of 'wild, abundant, extravagant forgiveness.'

references.name: Romans (Paul's letter)
references.context: Paul 'exhorts readers in his letter to the Romans' regarding suspension of judgment in the context of forgiveness.

references.context: Jesus demands that Peter forgive 'not just seven times, but 77 times' (or 'seventy times seven' in King James Version), the central biblical text structuring the sermon.

references.name: Matthew 18:23-35 (Parable of the Unforgiving Slave)
references.context: Jesus' parable about a king who shows merciful forgiveness to a slave, who then refuses to extend forgiveness to those who owe him; illustrates the demand for extravagant forgiveness.

references.name: Matthew 18:15-17 (Jesus' instructions on handling conflict)

references.name: Matthew 18:20 ('Where two or three are gathered in my name')
references.context: Cited to show Jesus' promise of presence even in the midst of community conflict.

references.name: The Lord's Prayer ('Forgive us our trespasses as we forgive those who trespass against us')
references.context: Preacher challenges listeners: 'Do we take to heart the words we say each week, forgive our trespasses as we forgive those who trespass against us?'

references.name: Anabaptist tradition (five centuries of)
references.context: Forgiveness is 'embedded in their [Amish] society through five centuries of Anabaptist tradition' and grounded in belief that life in Christ demands forgiveness.

references.name: Nickel Mines Schoolhouse shooting (October 2, 2006)
references.context: Historical event in Lancaster County, Pennsylvania; serves as the primary narrative case study demonstrating lived Christian forgiveness through Amish community response.

references.name: Restorative justice framework (implicit)
references.context: The sermon distinguishes between 'forgiveness' and 'pardon,' and explains that 'forgiveness leads to reconciliation, and reconciliation restores relationship within a community of a victim and an offender,' reflecting restorative rather than punitive justice philosophy.

primary_theme: Christian forgiveness is not a sentimental or optional practice but a foundational spiritual discipline grounded in Scripture and exemplified by the Amish community's response to the 2006 Nickel Mines shooting, calling believers to practice radical, repeated forgiveness ('77 times') as a response to God's forgiveness of us.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection; the preacher uses a detailed personal-historical narrative (the 2006 shooting and Amish response) as the primary rhetorical device to ground theological reflection on biblical texts, creating an emotional and ethical appeal that moves from concrete example to scriptural principle to moral challenge.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel / Gospel of Matthew
references.context: The preacher preached on 'this same text from Matthew's Gospel' six years prior, and returns to it as the lectionary cycles through readings every three years.

references.name: Matthew 14 (Walking on Water narrative)
references.context: The primary text: Jesus walking on water, the disciples in the boat, Peter attempting to walk on water, the stilling of the storm.

references.name: Matthew 8 (Jesus calming the storm)
references.context: The disciples had already seen Jesus quiet a storm in chapter 8 and wondered 'who could he possibly be that the wind and the sea obey him?'

references.name: Matthew 27 (Centurion at the cross)
references.context: The disciples' pronouncement 'truly you are the Son of God' foreshadows the centurion's words at the foot of the cross in Matthew 27.

references.name: Book of Job (Job chapters 38-41, Job 9:8)
references.context: Matthew's audience would have recognized 'reverberations of Job in this story'—Job declares that God 'trampled on the waves of the sea' and God questions Job about walking 'in the recesses of the deep,' establishing Jesus' identity with divine power over creation.

references.name: Mustard seed parable / Matthew 13:31-32
references.context: The preacher alludes to the mustard seed teaching: 'even when we feel like we only have a little faith...that can be enough...just like that mustard seed.'

references.name: Miraculous feeding of the 5,000
references.context: The disciples encounter Jesus walking on water 'immediately following the miraculous feeding of the 5,000' and 'still aren't convinced' of Jesus' identity.

references.name: Lectionary tradition
references.context: The preacher references the three-year lectionary cycle: 'as our lectionary readings come about every three years' and 'the same gospel lesson that comes around every three years.'

references.name: Richard [surname unknown]
references.context: The preacher quotes Richard saying 'no one ever walks out of church humming the sermon,' used humorously to suggest sermons are not memorable.

references.name: Etymology of 'nave' from Latin 'navis' (ship)
references.context: The preacher explains 'There's a reason why where we are is called the nave...from the Latin word for ship' and notes 'the ship or boat is an ancient symbol of the church.'

references.name: Ecclesiology (theology of the church as community/boat)
references.context: The sermon develops an ecclesiological understanding of the church as a boat in which the faithful stay together through storms, drawing on ancient Christian symbolism.

primary_theme: The sermon celebrates the faithful community of St. John's who, like the disciples in the boat, have stayed together through storms and uncertainty, while emphasizing Jesus' patient grace and continued presence with those who doubt.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses personal experience (six-year anniversary of first sermon), congregational memory (the storms of 2017), and scholarly biblical analysis (allusions to Job, Matthew's literary structure) to build theological insight about community resilience and Jesus' compassion.

references.name: Paul's Letter to the Romans
references.context: The preacher reflects on Paul's idea of adoption in today's text from Romans when pondering the concept of chosen family.

references.name: Romans 8:15 (implied: Abba, Father)
references.context: The preacher lists familial language from the passage: 'Abba, father, heirs, children, adoption.'

references.name: Romans 8:17 (implied: co-heirs with Christ)
references.context: The preacher emphasizes that through adoption 'we are joint heirs with Christ.'

references.name: Matthew 12:46-50 (implied: Jesus redefines family)
references.context: The preacher references Jesus telling his mother and brothers that his followers are his family when they are obstacles to his ministry.

references.name: John 19:26-27 (implied: Jesus and beloved disciple)
references.context: The preacher notes that from the cross, Jesus united the beloved disciple and Mary as mother and son.

references.name: Gospel passages on familial language
references.context: The preacher states that 'Jesus constantly used familial language to talk about and to his followers, brothers and sisters, siblings.'

references.name: Romans 6:3-4 (implied: baptism language)
references.context: The preacher references baptism: 'Through the waters of baptism, we are buried with him in his death, and we share in his resurrection.'

references.name: Sociology and Anthropology (discipline)
references.context: The preacher notes that 'sociologists and anthropologists call it [found family], chosen family' and references 'experts who study family structures and systems.'

references.name: Don Richardson
references.context: A Canadian missionary cited as an example of missionary work who went to Indonesia in the 1960s to evangelize headhunters and adapted his approach to local cultural understanding.

references.name: Peace Child
references.context: The preacher references this book as 'an account of his [Don Richardson's] time in Indonesia and his missionary achievement in spreading the good news of Christ to a remote corner of the world in a most unusual way.'

references.name: Missiology
references.context: The preacher identifies missiology as 'that study of the missionary work of the church' and references Don Richardson's story as an example of missionary adaptation.

references.name: Hebrew linguistic history
references.context: The preacher notes that ancient Hebrew people had no word for adoption until the 20th century, establishing historical context for Paul's rhetorical choice.

references.name: Ancient Roman family law and culture
references.context: The preacher explains that adoption was central to Roman culture, with adopted children receiving rights and protections, and that inheritance was conceptualized as a present reality, not posthumous.

primary_theme: Through the theological concept of adoption in Paul's letter to Romans, God offers all believers—especially Gentiles and those outside the biological covenant—full membership in God's chosen family with all attendant rights, inheritances, and belonging, mirroring the ancient Roman understanding of adoption as a complete identity transformation.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with scholarly argument: the preacher weaves personal narrative (sabbatical, chosen family experiences) with academic frameworks (sociological/anthropological terminology, historical analysis of Hebrew and Roman family structures), employs a missionary narrative (Don Richardson in Indonesia) as an analogy for Paul's evangelistic strategy, and concludes with congregational invitation and theological affirmation.

references.name: The Lion King
references.context: Opening narrative about young William watching the film and physically retreating from its frightening scenes while remaining transfixed by the story.

references.name: Scar (The Lion King character)
references.context: Referenced as 'that dastardly usurper scar' whose dramatic scenes trigger William's physical retreat.

references.name: The Eucharist
references.context: Mentioned as part of Maundy Thursday (the night before Good Friday), establishing Jesus' commandment to love one another.

references.name: Foot washing
references.context: Referenced as the 'tenderness of washing each other's feet,' representing humility and service on Maundy Thursday.

references.name: Godly Play
references.context: Referenced twice as a method of asking 'wondering questions' that prompt honest self-reflection about one's place in the Easter narrative.

references.name: Were You There? (spiritual)
references.context: The 'haunting spiritual' that poses the central wondering question: 'I wonder where you are in this story.'

references.name: Peter's denial and the cutting off of Malchus' ear
references.context: Peter is presented as impetuous—acting bravely and protectively by cutting off Malchus' ear, then denying Jesus three times within hours.

references.name: Pontius Pilate
references.context: Represents avoidance of moral responsibility by passing blame to others while allowing something known to be wrong.

references.name: The crowd at the crucifixion
references.context: Described as 'worked into a lather and rabid for crucifixion,' representing collective moral failure.

references.name: Mary, mother of Jesus
references.context: One of the three Marys at the crucifixion who found strength to stay and witness despite heartbreak.

references.name: Mary Magdalene
references.context: One of the three Marys at the crucifixion who remained faithful witnesses to Jesus' death.

references.name: Mary, wife of Clopas
references.context: One of the three Marys at the crucifixion who stood with the beloved disciple and Jesus' mother.

references.name: The beloved disciple
references.context: Present at the crucifixion with the three Marys, representing faithful witness.

references.name: Joseph of Arimathea
references.context: Prepared Jesus' body and laid it in the tomb while pondering Jesus' mysterious words about rebirth and eternal life.

references.name: Nicodemus
references.context: Assisted Joseph of Arimathea in preparing Jesus' body, representing those still pondering the meaning of Jesus' teaching in darkness.

references.name: The Crucifixion
references.context: The central event—Jesus on the cross—that the sermon argues cannot be looked away from or bypassed in the Christian narrative.

references.name: The Resurrection
references.context: Referenced as impossible without the crucifixion; the vanquishing of death that follows Good Friday.

references.name: Easter Vigil/Sacramental rites
references.context: Referenced as the moment when 'all the lights suddenly come on' and 'the alleluias begin again' after Holy Saturday.

references.name: Narrative theology
references.context: The entire sermon operates within a narrative framework that sees Good Friday as an essential but painful part of a larger redemptive arc.

primary_theme: Good Friday demands unflinching witness to Christ's suffering and death as an inseparable, necessary part of the greater Christian narrative of redemption, rather than a painful chapter to be avoided or bypassed.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection using personal anecdote (William and The Lion King) as an extended metaphor to guide listeners through self-examination about their own role in the Passion story, employing the Godly Play methodology of wondering questions to create intimate theological engagement.

references.name: John 3:16
references.context: For God so loved the world that He gave His only Son, so that everyone who believes in Him may not perish, but may have eternal life—cited as the theological frame for Jesus's encounter with the Samaritan woman.

references.name: John 4 (Samaritan woman at the well)
references.context: The entire gospel lesson for the Third Sunday in Lent, featuring Jesus's conversation with the Samaritan woman—the longest conversation Jesus has with anyone in the Gospels.

references.name: John 4:26 ('I am he')
references.context: Jesus's first 'I am' statement in John's Gospel, given directly to the Samaritan woman with no riddles or obscure language.

references.name: Nicodemus (John 3)
references.context: Referenced as one of the Lenten gospel lessons featuring Jesus meeting with an individual (at night), contrasted with the Samaritan woman's daytime encounter.

references.name: Man blind from birth (John 9)
references.context: Named as an upcoming Lenten gospel lesson featuring Jesus's personal encounter with an individual.

references.name: Martha and Lazarus (John 11)
references.context: Referenced as the concluding Lenten gospel lesson, where Martha questions Jesus about his tardiness and Lazarus's death.

references.name: Jacob (Jewish/Samaritan ancestor)
references.context: The Samaritan woman invokes their common ancestor Jacob when asking if Jesus is greater than the giver of the well.

references.name: First disciples (fishing nets and plows)
references.context: Used as analogy for how the Samaritan woman drops her water jar and runs to evangelize, parallel to disciples leaving their livelihoods.

references.name: Samaritan-Jewish religious conflict
references.context: The preacher notes that for centuries Samaritans and Jews occupied neighboring lands, practiced similar religions, but expressed animosity and disagreed about where and how God should be worshipped.

references.name: Catechumenal/EFM (Education for Ministry) tradition
references.context: The preacher invokes the Anglican/Episcopal educational tradition, suggesting Jesus functions as a catechumenate teacher or EFM mentor in his dialogue with the Samaritan woman.

references.name: Episcopalian practice and theology
references.context: The sermon's playful argument that Jesus demonstrates Episcopalian traits through his theological dialogue, intellectual openness, and invitation for questioning.

references.name: Lenten spiritual practice and reflection
references.context: The sermon frames Lent as a time for personal examination, expanding understanding, identifying spiritual encumbrances, and deepening awareness of God's presence.

primary_theme: During Lent, Jesus meets individuals personally and invites them into theological dialogue, teaching through question and encounter rather than proclamation; the Samaritan woman exemplifies openness to God's interruption, engagement across difference, and the willingness to abandon hindrances in order to witness to the gospel.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with playful intellectual argumentation, using narrative exegesis, personal theological questioning, and homiletical analogy to invite congregational self-examination and spiritual practice.

references.name: Eeyore
references.context: Preacher describes her son as 'a bit of an Eeyore' to characterize a pessimistic, glass-half-empty worldview.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: The entire sermon is structured around John 1:35-39, particularly Jesus's question 'What are you looking for?' and the call to 'Come and see.'

references.name: John 1:29, 35-39
references.context: Preacher cites John the Baptist's declaration 'Here is the Lamb of God' and the disciples' question to Jesus 'Where are you staying?' (abiding/remaining).

references.name: John's 'I am' statements
references.context: Preacher notes that at the beginning of his ministry, Jesus does not yet make the 'I am' statements characteristic of the Gospel of John.

references.name: Magi / Nativity
references.context: Preacher references the story of the Magi following a star during Epiphany season as an example of seeking and looking for Jesus.

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Preacher cites Taylor's description of being 'a detective of divinity, collecting evidence of God's genius and admiring the tracks left for me to follow.'

references.name: Epiphany season / liturgical tradition
references.context: Preacher frames the sermon within the liturgical season of Epiphany, which asks about revelation and seeking who Jesus is.

references.name: Catechumenate
references.context: Preacher mentions the catechumenate process (second round with 17 newcomers, plus 18 from Broderick's group in October) as a lived example of people looking and coming to abide.

primary_theme: Jesus invites seekers to 'come and see' and abide with him rather than being convinced or sold on a predetermined experience; true evangelism asks 'What are you looking for?' instead of presuming to know others' needs.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral self-reflection; the preacher uses personal anecdotes (her son Owen, the rodeo parade) to explore her own tendency to oversell experiences, then contrasts this with Jesus's gentler, question-based approach in the Gospel of John, concluding with practical application for the congregation's mission and spiritual lives.

references.name: Isaiah 2:2-4
references.context: "In days to come, the mountain of the Lord's house shall be established... People shall beat their swords into plowshares and their spears into pruning hooks... Nations shall not lift up sword against nation, neither shall they learn war anymore."

references.context: First chapter shows people lamenting they have broken everything; Chapter 2 offers God's restoration: "Come, let us walk in the light of the Lord."

references.name: Pete Seeger
references.context: Preacher references Seeger's revival of a spiritual during the Vietnam era, noting the song "Down by the Riverside" has been in her head all week.

references.name: "Down by the Riverside" (spiritual/song)
references.context: A beautiful spiritual revived during the Vietnam era by Pete Seeger, running through the preacher's head all week.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel
references.context: The lectionary for the upcoming 12 months; Matthew's particular bent includes "discernment, judgment, watchfulness, and the surprising presence of God."

references.name: Eucharistic liturgy / Mystery of Faith
references.context: "Christ has died, Christ is risen, Christ will come again. But not yet." Invoked to illustrate the "in-between time."

references.name: The Collect (Anglican liturgy)
references.context: The collect this morning speaks to the mortal life between Jesus's first coming in humility and his second coming in majesty.

references.name: The Lord's Prayer
references.context: Reference to a child reverently reciting the Lord's Prayer during worship.

references.name: Advent theology / season
references.context: Advent as a theological season reminding us we live in darkness awaiting light, in a promised but not-yet-realized time; the season teaches watchfulness as a posture of hope.

references.name: The Nativity tradition (positioning of baby Jesus figure)
references.context: Reference to the tradition of waiting until Christmas to place baby Jesus in the nativity scene, which the preacher intentionally subverts this year.

references.name: Eucharistic theology (one bread, one cup)
references.context: "We who are many are one body, because we all share one bread, one cup" - invoking 1 Corinthians imagery of ecclesial unity.

references.name: Doom scrolling (implicit cultural critique)
references.context: The preacher notes that when she can stop doom scrolling and look away from screens, she sees hope and God's presence.

primary_theme: In the liturgical season of Advent—the in-between time of waiting between Christ's first coming and his return—we are called to maintain watchfulness and hope amid darkness, recognizing God's surprising presence even as we face contemporary spiritual heaviness and violence.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection blended with prophetic challenge: the preacher opens with personal narrative and vulnerability, transitions to lament about recent tragedies, grounds the response theologically in Advent and Isaiah, and concludes by identifying concrete signs of hope within the congregation's life together.

references.context: Attributed author of 2 Timothy, though authorship is contested by scholars; Paul is presented as a model of community-oriented ministry and spiritual mentorship.

references.context: The preacher notes she has preached from other texts three times in a row rather than from Luke, though she affirms liking Luke.

references.context: Institution where Vincent Harding taught for over 20 years; represents academic theological education.

rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative with scholarly support; uses personal anecdotes (grandmother stories, Vincent Harding's icebreaker exercise), scholarly research (Lindsay Harden Freeman's Bible study), biblical typology (testament literature), and direct application to congregational life to invite listeners into deeper understanding of spiritual kinship and communal faith.

references.name: 2 Timothy

references.name: Apostle Paul
references.context: Attributed author of 2 Timothy (though Pearson notes scholarly debate about authorship); Paul as theological authority on community, adoption, and faith.

references.context: Biblical example of testament literature: 'Moses did it throughout Deuteronomy.'

references.name: Solomon
references.context: Biblical example of testament literature: 'The wise Solomon gave practical political advice just before his death.'
