references.name: Lectionary tradition
references.context: The preacher notes that 'passages from this wisdom text are rarely appearing in our lectionary, I think half a dozen or fewer times over the course of the three years.'

references.name: Idioms and proverbs as cultural signposts
references.context: The sermon opens with the observation that 'Proverbs are a cultural signpost, an indication of what a society values and sees as normative, and they're clues to everyday living.'

references.name: Humility as virtue in Scripture
references.context: The preacher emphasizes: 'Humility is a virtue that is prized throughout Scripture, and especially so in Proverbs. It is understanding that humility is to be sought and lived because all wisdom belongs to God, and we are not God.'

references.name: Sacredness of ordinary life
references.context: The preacher develops the theme that 'there is a sacredness in the most ordinary encounters on the most ordinary of days,' drawing from the scholarly observation about Proverbs.

references.name: Theology of action and authentic faith
references.context: The sermon emphasizes that 'true religion is action and bring forth in us the fruit of good works' and that 'our actions reflect our values.'

references.name: Ordinary Time (liturgical season)
references.context: The preacher notes that 'Proverbs are the wisdom of ordinary day-to-day life, perfect for this season of ordinary time,' referencing the liturgical calendar.

references.name: The Twelfth Sunday after Pentecost (liturgical context)
references.context: This is the sermon's title and liturgical context, indicating placement within the ecclesiastical calendar.

primary_theme: The Book of Proverbs teaches how to live faithfully in community through humble actions that reveal our character and reflect God's values in the ordinary moments of daily life.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling combined with scholarly literary analysis; the preacher uses personal anecdotes (particularly about her husband's humility as a chocolate company owner) to illustrate biblical wisdom, integrating close textual reading of Proverbs with practical theological reflection on virtue and authentic faith.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: The preacher has been 'journeying with Luke since December' and identifies hospitality as one of the main themes throughout the gospel.

references.name: Luke 10:38-42 (Mary and Martha story)
references.context: The entire sermon is an exegetical examination of this gospel reading about two sisters and their encounter with Jesus.

references.name: Luke 10:1-12 (disciples welcomed into homes)
references.context: Referenced as an example of the hospitality theme in Luke: 'We saw it two weeks ago when the disciples were welcomed into homes to share God's peace.'

references.name: Luke 10:25-37 (Good Samaritan)
references.context: Referenced as another example of hospitality in Luke: 'We saw it again last week when the good Samaritan took care of the injured man on the road.'

references.name: Luke 11:5-8 (parable of the friend at midnight)
references.context: Preacher notes they will see hospitality again 'next week' when Jesus tells of 'a man who will...give bread to a friend at the door, even though it is the middle of the night.'

references.name: Madeleine L'Engle
references.context: Contemporary Christian writer and poet cited as the author of a poem about Martha that reframes her story.

references.name: Madeleine L'Engle, 'Martha' (poem)
references.context: A short poem recited at the sermon's conclusion that gives voice to Martha's dignity and essential role: 'I was the one who baked the bread. I pressed the grapes for wine.'

references.name: Parable as literary form
references.context: The preacher analyzes Mary and Martha as 'a living parable' with exaggerated foils and open-ended conclusions that invite identification, similar to Jesus's parables.

references.name: Work-life balance
references.context: The preacher jokes 'Maybe Jesus was the first one to come up with that phrase, work-life balance' when discussing the need for balance between action and contemplation.

references.name: Contemplative and active life (vita contemplativa vs. vita activa)
references.context: The sermon employs the classical Christian distinction between lives of contemplation and action, suggesting both are necessary for spiritual wholeness.

references.name: Theological imagination
references.context: The preacher invokes 'theological imagination' as a method of biblical interpretation: 'I love it when we engage our theological imaginations and come at these stories from a different direction.'

references.name: Lectionary
references.context: The sermon discusses a 'lectionary study' led by a parishioner and references the preacher's engagement with lectionary-based teaching and preaching.

primary_theme: The Mary and Martha story teaches that a balanced Christian life requires both contemplative reception and active service, challenging the false dichotomy that has historically assigned women to rigid roles.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling, employing scholarly biblical analysis (genre study, thematic analysis, comparative reading) alongside personal anecdotes and imaginative re-reading of a familiar text to invite listeners into theological questioning and self-examination.

references.name: Psalm 46:10
references.context: The theme of the retreat was based on 'Be still and know that I am God' from Psalm 46.

references.name: Luke 8:22-39
references.context: The entire gospel lesson for the day, featuring Jesus calming the storm and healing the demon-possessed man named Legion.

references.name: People magazine
references.context: Used as an example of second-hand knowledge: 'I can read all about celebrities in People magazine and follow them on Instagram, but that will never make them my friend.'

references.name: Sampertaro
references.context: Referenced for his teaching about 'the cinematic nature of Luke's gospel, the special effects and the heightened drama compared to some of the other gospels' during Lent.

references.name: Roman military terminology (Legion as 6,000 troops)
references.context: Historical context explaining that 'a legion was a Roman military designation for 6,000 troops, 6,000 hostile occupiers' to illuminate meaning for Luke's original audience.

references.name: Godly Play pedagogy
references.context: The preacher uses the Godly Play question 'I wonder where you are in this story and what part of this story is about you' as an interpretive tool.

references.name: Episcopal intellectual and rational Christianity
references.context: The preacher notes that 'as Episcopalians, we like to think of ourselves as intellectual and rational Christians,' suggesting some resistance to personal spiritual experience narratives.

references.name: Person-first and identity-affirming language movements
references.context: The preacher references contemporary movements to humanize people: 'person living with cancer,' 'people experiencing homelessness,' and saying names of those killed by unjust violence.

references.name: Incarnational theology / Restoration of humanity
references.context: The emphasis that 'We are always more than what ails us' and Jesus restores identity and full humanity reflects incarnational theological anthropology.

primary_theme: Personal experiential knowledge of God through direct encounter and relationship, rather than secondhand observation, is essential to faith, illustrated through the Gospel story of Legion's healing and restoration to wholeness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling, using personal anecdote (the retreat), contemporary examples (celebrity culture, homelessness, illness language), careful scriptural exegesis, and reflective questions to invite congregational self-examination.

references.name: New York Times article 'The Psychic Numbing of Mass Tragedies'
references.context: Opening reference that introduces the sermon's central concept and resonated with the preacher 'on a deep level, to my very core.'

references.name: Dr. Paul Slovik
references.context: University of Oregon psychologist who named the phenomenon of psychic numbing and provided scientific explanation for emotional detachment in response to data and the sense of powerlessness before massive problems.

references.name: Nadia Boltzweber
references.context: Friend of the cathedral and occasional preacher who used the illustration of an old fuse box to explain how humans are not designed for the 24-hour news cycle and can 'blow a fuse and shut down' when overwhelmed.

references.name: Psalm 97
references.context: Today's psalm declaring 'The Lord is King. Righteousness and justice are the foundations of God's throne' and warning against worship of idols of dominance and destruction.

references.name: Psalm 22
references.context: The lament psalm Jesus quoted from the cross: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'

references.name: John Bell
references.context: Scottish minister, hymn writer affiliated with Iona, keynote speaker at Colorado clergy gathering who taught that the Psalms give us vocabulary for pain, frustration, anger, and anguish and were Jesus' primary vocabulary for prayer.

references.name: Living with the Psalms
references.context: John Bell's recently published book whose lectures on the Psalms shaped the preacher's understanding of how biblical lament can resist psychic numbing.

references.name: The Psalms (general tradition of lament)
references.context: Described as a tradition of prayers of lament that 'for thousands of years, the faithful have been crying out to God' with hard questions and without sanitized language.

references.name: Ecclesiastes 1:9 ('There is very little that is new under the sun')
references.context: Implicit reference when the preacher states 'when you spend time with the Psalms, you quickly come to realize there is very little that is new under the sun.'

references.name: Christian theology of lamentation and prayer
references.context: The sermon invokes a tradition of honest, unfiltered prayer to God that does not require 'sanitized' or 'sweet' language and honors the full range of human emotion.

references.name: Christian practice of prayer and intercession
references.context: The preacher defends and reframes 'thoughts and prayers' as a Christian practice that activates believers against psychic numbing and connects them to God's justice.

references.name: Iona Community
references.context: Implicitly referenced through John Bell's affiliation; represents a tradition of liturgical reform and socially engaged Christian witness.

primary_theme: Christians can resist psychic numbing in response to mass tragedy through honest thoughts and prayers—particularly the vocabulary of lament found in the Psalms—which activate them to work for the righteousness and justice that are the foundation of God's kingdom.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with prophetic challenge; uses contemporary psychology (psychic numbing) as a bridge to biblical wisdom (the Psalms), integrating narrative illustration (the fuse box), expert testimony (Slovik, Bell), and scriptural exposition to move from intellectual understanding to emotional and spiritual activation.

references.name: Sister Macrina Whitaker
references.context: Benedictine nun cited as an authority on baptism and spiritual experience; her writing and poetry frame the theological meaning of baptism throughout the sermon.

references.name: A Tree Full of Angels
references.context: Book by Sister Macrina Whitaker quoted to provide mystical reflection on baptism and original possibility in human nature.

references.name: Frail and Glorious
references.context: Poem by Sister Macrina Whitaker used as the structural and thematic anchor for the sermon's exploration of baptismal identity and divine recognition.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: The Thomas pericope (John 20:24-29) forms the primary scriptural text for the Second Sunday of Easter and the sermon's opening meditation.

references.name: John 20:26
references.context: "Although the doors were shut, Jesus came and stood among them and said, Peace be with you"—quoted to connect Jesus's appearance to Thomas with the spirit of baptism.

references.name: Mary Magdalene
references.context: Referenced as a biblical figure whose witness to the resurrection was doubted by the disciples, used to contextualize Thomas's skepticism within broader narrative of doubt.

references.name: Peter and the beloved disciple
references.context: Referenced as characters who also doubted and needed to see the tomb for themselves, challenging the unique stigma placed on Thomas.

references.name: The Baptismal Covenant
references.context: Episcopal Church liturgical practice cited as the framework for recommitment to faith and the covenant's specific promises regarding justice, peace, and human dignity.

references.name: Easter Vigil
references.context: Identified as one of the designated baptismal feasts in the Episcopal Church tradition, with explanation of St. John's practice of moving baptisms to the Sunday after Easter.

references.name: The Thanksgiving over Water
references.context: Episcopal liturgical text quoted verbatim to articulate the theological meaning of baptism: burial with Christ, sharing in resurrection, and rebirth through the Holy Spirit.

references.name: Original sin
references.context: Referenced implicitly in Sister Macrina's poem when she writes that God's eye beheld the baptized as "infinite possibility" rather than inherent sinfulness.

references.name: Sacramental theology
references.context: Implicit throughout as the sermon frames baptism as a repeatable, community-affirming sacramental act that renews covenant and identity.

references.name: Mystical theology / Benedictine spirituality
references.context: The heavy reliance on Sister Macrina's poetic, contemplative language about divine union, original possibility, and spiritual awareness reflects medieval and contemporary mystical thought.

primary_theme: Baptism is a sacramental moment of divine affirmation and human choice in which we are reminded of our original union with God and invited to recommit to covenant faith, much as Thomas chose belief when Jesus appeared to him.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection grounded in liturgical tradition, using poetic citation (Sister Macrina), biblical reframing (Thomas's doubt contextualized), and congregational address to invite personal and communal renewal.

references.name: Sam Pertaro
references.context: Dean's Forum speaker who highlighted the departure of John's account from synoptic Gospels' treatment of the Last Supper, emphasizing foot-washing over Eucharist.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: John's Maundy Thursday reading emphasizes foot-washing as the central action rather than the Supper, departing from synoptic accounts.

references.name: Synoptic Gospels (Matthew, Mark, Luke)
references.context: Contrasted with John's account; in synoptic Gospels the Last Supper is the central action of Maundy Thursday.

references.name: 1 Corinthians
references.context: Paul's letter addressing class divisions in the Corinthian community during communion—wealthy arriving early and consuming food before poor members arrive.

references.name: John 13:14-15
references.context: Direct quotation: 'So if I, your Lord and teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet. For I have set you an example that you should do as I have done to you.'

references.name: Bishop Robb
references.context: Referenced as frequently articulating the concept of 're-membering' the community, the body of Christ, in relation to communion.

references.name: Primitive Baptist Church tradition
references.context: Historical practice of combining foot-washing with communion to maintain humility and prevent preferential treatment based on wealth.

references.name: Christian Eucharistic theology
references.context: The theological framework explaining why Eucharist became central to Christian practice, possibly due to 'three gospels against one' or aesthetic preferences.

primary_theme: Foot-washing and communion should be practiced together to maintain Christian community equality and humility, challenging class-based divisions that undermine the sacrament's meaning.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral-prophetic reflection combining scholarly biblical analysis with practical congregational example, using rhetorical questions to challenge current practice and invite imaginative reimagining of church tradition.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: The primary text under discussion; the anointing story in John 12 featuring Mary of Bethany anointing Jesus' feet.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Referenced as one of four Gospel accounts of the anointing story, occurring in the house of Simon the leper with disciples objecting to the waste.

references.name: Gospel of Mark
references.context: Another version of the anointing story paralleling Matthew's account, with Simon the leper and disciples protesting the waste of costly ointment.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Features an unnamed sinful woman anointing Jesus at Simon the Pharisee's dinner; the protest concerns her status as a sinner rather than wastefulness.

references.name: John 2 (Wedding at Cana)
references.context: Jesus' first miracle in John's gospel, turning water into wine in abundance; cited as exemplifying the theme of abundance in John's theology.

references.name: John 10:10
references.context: Jesus as good shepherd telling disciples he came 'that they may have life and have it abundantly'; establishes abundance as a Johannine theme.

references.name: John 12 (Lazarus resurrection)
references.context: The resurrection of Lazarus occurring 'just a chapter ago' before the anointing story; creates intertextual connection between death and life themes.

references.name: John 13 (Foot washing)
references.context: Alluded to when noting that Mary's wiping of Jesus' feet prefigures Jesus wiping his disciples' feet.

references.name: Ash Wednesday / Lenten liturgy
references.context: Referenced regarding the wearing of ashes on foreheads at the start of Lent and the dismissal formula 'Remember the poor.'

references.name: Pope Gregory I
references.context: Cited as the historical source of the conflation of Mary Magdalene with the sinful woman in Luke and Mary of Bethany; this conflation occurred in an Easter sermon in the year 951.

references.name: Saturday Night Live
references.context: The preacher compares her reflection on Mary's anointing to a 'Saturday Night Live skit,' invoking contemporary comedic sensibility to contextualize clumsiness and accident.

references.name: Doctrine of papal infallibility
references.context: The preacher quips 'Perhaps popes are not infallible' in response to Gregory I's historical error regarding Mary Magdalene's identity.

references.name: Mary Magdalene as repentant prostitute tradition
references.context: The sermon addresses and corrects the long-standing tradition that Mary Magdalene was a prostitute or sinner, tracing this misidentification to Pope Gregory I's 10th-century sermon.

primary_theme: Mary of Bethany's deliberate and extravagant anointing of Jesus embodies the abundant, lavish devotion central to John's gospel, challenging listeners to examine how they express their faith overtly and generously.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with textual scholarship; the preacher uses personal anecdote (her own clumsiness with household tasks) as a humorous entry point, then pivots to careful comparative analysis of the four Gospel accounts, historical correction of the Mary Magdalene tradition, and theological reflection on Johannine themes of abundance.

references.name: Genesis 15:1
references.context: "Do not be afraid, Abram. I am your shield." - Opening comfort text for the sermon.

references.name: Psalm 27
references.context: God described as "our light and our salvation, the strength of our life" who shelters us in trouble.

references.name: Luke 13:31-35
references.context: Central Gospel passage: Jesus as a hen desiring to gather Jerusalem's children under her wings, contrasted with Herod as a fox.

references.name: Luke 9:51
references.context: Referenced as the point where "Jesus set his face to go to Jerusalem," anchoring the Lenten journey.

references.name: Hosea (she-bear imagery)
references.context: God likened to "an enraged she-bear" as a fierce female image for God.

references.name: Deuteronomy (mother eagle imagery)
references.context: God described as "a soaring mother eagle," part of comparison of fierce female divine images.

references.name: Aesop
references.context: Author of fables used as intellectual shorthand for animal symbolism; specifically referenced for how animals convey deeper meaning across cultures.

references.name: Aesop's Fables (The Fox and the Hen)
references.context: A specific fable (attributed to Aesop) about a hen outwitting a fox through cleverness is cited as parallel to Jesus's interaction with Herod.

references.name: The English teaching tradition
references.context: Preacher identifies herself as "ever the former English teacher" to justify literary analysis of biblical symbolism.

references.name: Animal symbolism in literature
references.context: The tradition of using animals as shorthand for character traits (e.g., fox = slyness) is invoked to explain the Herod/hen contrast.

primary_theme: God's protective, maternal care (imaged as a hen sheltering her brood) offers comfort and refuge amid contemporary crises, and Jesus embodies this humble, persistent work of healing and gathering despite opposition.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with literary analysis; the preacher uses personal narrative, contemporary context (pandemic, Ukraine war), playful scholarly digression (rabbit holes, Google searches), and imaginative reinterpretation of biblical imagery through fable and animal symbolism to make ancient texts speak to modern anxieties.

references.name: Book of Jeremiah
references.context: Jeremiah's call story from the Old Testament, where God appoints him as a prophet to the nations despite his reluctance, saying 'I am only a boy.'

references.name: Book of Isaiah
references.context: Isaiah's apocalyptic vision of the Lord on a throne with seraphs singing 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts. The whole earth is full of His glory.'

references.name: Isaiah 6 (Isaiah's Call Vision)
references.context: The detailed vision of Isaiah seeing the Lord on a high and lofty throne, the hem of his robe filling the temple, seraphs with six wings, and a seraph touching Isaiah's lips with a hot coal.

references.name: Jeremiah 1:4-10 (Jeremiah's Call)
references.context: God tells Jeremiah not to say 'I am only a boy,' commands him to speak God's words, and touches his mouth, putting God's words in his mouth.

references.name: Hebrew Scripture scholarship
references.context: The preacher cites scholarly interpretation that the throne is associated with the Holy of Holies and the temple represents the whole of creation.

references.name: 'The Lord of Sea and Sky' (Catholic liturgical hymn/song)
references.context: A 1980s Catholic liturgical song made the exchange 'Whom shall I send? Here I am, send me' popular and it is still used at many ordinations.

references.name: Sanctus/Holy, Holy, Holy (Liturgical tradition)
references.context: The preacher references both the biblical version ('Holy, holy, holy is the Lord of hosts') and the liturgical version ('Holy, holy, holy, God of power and might') sung weekly in worship.

references.name: Christian ordination/discernment tradition
references.context: The preacher discusses how call stories are central to discernment processes for those seeking ordained ministry, citing the experience of three postulants in the congregation.

references.name: Episcopalian theology and practice
references.context: The preacher notes that Episcopalians are not accustomed to speaking about being 'called by God' in the way other traditions do, making such language feel awkward.

references.name: Theology of baptismal ministry
references.context: The preacher asserts that 'We all have a ministry as the baptized' and that call stories should not be exclusive to the ordained.

references.name: St. John's Cathedral (architectural/liturgical reference)
references.context: Used as a concrete example to help the congregation imagine the scale of Isaiah's vision ('In case you are imagining something on the scale of St. John's Cathedral...') and later mentions smoke from incense at their own cathedral.

primary_theme: God's call to service is extended to all Christians through baptism, not just to ordained clergy, and all are invited to respond with availability and commitment to God's work in the world.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining biblical narrative exegesis with personal testimony, using conversational language and direct address to challenge congregational assumptions while celebrating communal commitment to ministry.

references.name: Nancy Drew
references.context: Preacher mentions 'Nancy Drew, I love a mystery thing' to explain her affinity for the Epiphany season as one focused on discovery and clues.

references.name: Aristotelian triptych
references.context: Preacher identifies Luke's gospel structure as following the Aristotelian triptych formula: 'Tell them what you're going to tell them. Tell them. Tell them what you told them.'

references.name: Luke 1 - The Magnificat (Mary's Song)
references.context: Quoted to show Mary's prophetic announcement that Jesus 'has shown strength with his arm' and 'has brought down the powerful from their thrones and lifted up the lowly.'

references.name: Isaiah (unnamed passage)
references.context: Jesus reads from Isaiah in the synagogue, proclaiming 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he has anointed me to bring good news to the poor.'

references.name: Luke 4:18-19 (Jesus' Nazareth Sermon)
references.context: The primary text: Jesus announces 'good news to the poor,' 'release to the captives,' 'recovery of sight to the blind,' and 'the year of the Lord's favor.'

references.name: John's 'I am' statements
references.context: Preacher notes that Luke's version uses Jesus' declaration 'today this scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing' as Luke's equivalent to John's more direct 'I am' statements.

references.name: Gospel of Luke (entire narrative arc)
references.context: The sermon traces how Mary's prophetic words and Jesus' inaugural sermon announcement will be 'reminded of...throughout Luke' in stories of healing, teaching, and releasing.

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Quoted for the concept of becoming 'detectives of the divine' as the invitation of the Epiphany season.

references.name: An Altar in the World
references.context: Barbara Brown Taylor's work is the source of the metaphor that Christians are invited to become 'detectives of the divine.'

references.name: The Epiphany season (liturgical tradition)
references.context: The sermon frames Epiphany as a season of 'wonder and discovery' where stories reveal Jesus' divinity and humanity through 'miracles and teachings.'

references.name: Wedding at Cana (John 2)
references.context: Briefly mentioned as last week's Gospel reading that taught about 'a mother and son relationship' through Jesus' first miracle.

primary_theme: The Epiphany season invites us to become detectives of the divine by collecting and interpreting Gospel stories that reveal who Jesus is—just as personal stories reveal our true character and priorities.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection; the preacher opens with a personal, humorous anecdote about forgetting her son's name on Christmas cards, uses that as a parallel structure to explain how Jesus' stories reveal his identity, and closes with an invitation to spiritual practice rather than doctrinal argument.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel (Matthew 2:13-23)
references.context: The primary text for the sermon: Joseph's dream warning, the Holy Family's flight to Egypt, and Herod's slaughter of the innocents.

references.name: Luke 2 (Shepherd and Heavenly Host narrative)
references.context: Referenced as contrast to Matthew's narrative: 'the shepherds have returned to their flocks in the fields. The heavenly host, singing glory to God in the highest, has moved on to other angelic duties.'

references.name: Ash Wednesday liturgy
references.context: The penitential Christian tradition of ashes and the phrase 'ashes to ashes, dust to dust' as acknowledgment of mortality.

references.name: Emmanuel
references.context: Invoked as a season of hope: 'a season of hope and Emmanuel, a Christmas star.'

references.name: The Incarnation (God made flesh)
references.context: Theological concept invoked when describing the precarious nature of life: 'It's a stark reminder of the precarious nature of life, even in the midst of miraculous incarnation.'

references.name: Ann Jones's book on stained glass windows
references.context: Consulted to identify the Clara Story window as the New Testament children's window: 'thanks to Ann Jones' fabulous book about all of our stained glass windows, I didn't have to look far for my answer.'

references.name: Simeon (Luke 2:25-32)
references.context: Depicted in the stained glass window, 'holding the infant Jesus, flanked by Mary and Joseph.'

references.name: John the Baptist
references.context: Depicted in the stained glass window 'with his parents.'

references.name: Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:21-43, Luke 8:40-56)
references.context: Depicted in the stained glass window as 'Adult Jesus is also depicted with Jairus' daughter.'

references.name: The feeding of the five thousand (Mark 6:30-44, Matthew 14:13-21, Luke 9:10-17, John 6:1-14)
references.context: Referenced through the window's depiction of 'the child offering his precious few loaves and fish for the feeding of so many.'

references.name: The Holy Innocents (Matthew 2:16-18)
references.context: The martyred children under age two in Bethlehem, depicted as 'a row of nine children, nine little boys who would be no more than two years old.'

primary_theme: The paradoxical coexistence of suffering and joy in the Christian story, exemplified by finding a Christmas star on Ash Wednesday and by Matthew's account of the Holy Family's flight amid the slaughter of innocents, reminding us that redemption is real but precarious.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection with sacramental observation—the preacher uses a personal encounter with a glittered star in the cathedral as a concrete entry point into theological paradox, then deepens the reflection through biblical commentary and contemporary pastoral connection (evacuating families, mourning Canon Burt Womack), emphasizing the intertwining of liturgical seasons and human suffering.

references.name: Zephaniah (Book of)
references.context: Primary biblical text for the sermon; the preacher discusses Zephaniah's proclamation that 'God will rejoice over us with gladness and exult over us with loud singing' and describes it as containing 'some of the darkest images in scripture' before turning to joy.

references.name: Latin language / 'Gaudete' (rejoice)
references.context: The preacher opens by explaining that 'Gaudete means rejoice in Latin' and frames the entire sermon around this linguistic and liturgical concept.

references.name: Henry Nouwen
references.context: Cited as a theologian who 'distinguishes between happiness and joy in his book Here and Now,' defining happiness as dependent on external conditions and joy as knowing one is unconditionally loved.

references.name: Here and Now
references.context: Henry Nouwen's book cited for its distinction between happiness and joy, foundational to the sermon's theological argument about joy as a spiritual fruit rather than emotional pleasure.

references.name: Kate Bowler
references.context: Duke Divinity professor who hosts the podcast 'Everything Happens' and interviewed Gretchen Rubin about the difference between happiness and joy.

references.name: Gretchen Rubin
references.context: Author of The Happiness Project, interviewed on Kate Bowler's podcast; discussed the notion that joy feels like a gift, much like grace.

references.name: The Happiness Project
references.context: Work by Gretchen Rubin cited in the context of a podcast discussion about the difference between happiness and joy.

references.name: Everything Happens (podcast)
references.context: Kate Bowler's podcast where she interviewed Gretchen Rubin about happiness and joy; the preacher was 'struck by the moment in their conversation' about joy as gift.

references.name: Luke's Gospel
references.context: The preacher's women's Bible study has been examining 'the story of women in Luke's gospel for the past four months,' studying healing stories and what healing means.

references.name: Emmanuel / 'God with us'
references.context: The preacher uses the term 'Emmanuel' repeatedly as shorthand for God's presence and incarnation, drawing on Matthew 1:23 without explicit citation.

references.name: The Fruit of the Spirit (joy)
references.context: The preacher states 'joy, not happiness, is a fruit of the Spirit,' alluding to Galatians 5:22 and Christian theological tradition without explicit citation.

references.name: Incarnation / Crèche imagery
references.context: The preacher uses the broken crèche as a symbol of 'the incarnation, this familiar tableau reminding us that God was born of flesh to live among us,' invoking Christian doctrinal tradition.

references.name: Advent season / liturgical theology
references.context: The sermon situates itself within the Third Sunday of Advent (Gaudete Sunday), using liturgical colors (rose candle and vestments) and the church calendar to structure theological reflection.

references.name: Grace (theological concept)
references.context: The preacher equates joy with grace, describing both as 'something undeserved and unmitigated,' drawing on Protestant theological tradition without explicit attribution.

primary_theme: Joy as a spiritual fruit grounded in God's presence and unconditional love can sustain us through sorrow and hardship, transforming how we experience suffering.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combining narrative storytelling (personal anecdotes about family crisis and Bible study), scholarly theological argument (Nouwen and Rubin citations), and prophetic liturgical proclamation (repeated calls to 'Rejoice').

references.name: Kairos vs. Kronos time

references.context: Liturgical season of introspection: 'I wonder in this last week of Lent, this season of so much introspection.'

references.name: Irish greeting 'sláinte'

primary_theme: God's radical forgiveness includes not only pardoning sin but also forgetting it entirely, liberating believers from the burden of shame and enabling full restoration of relationship.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with scholarly support; blends personal narrative (St. Patrick's Day recollection, Irish heritage), philosophical argumentation (Locke on identity), theological analysis (covenant theology), artistic/cultural references (Michelangelo, Rembrandt, Chagall), and prophetic questioning to invite congregational introspection during Lent.

references.name: Genesis 1

references.name: Genesis 13

references.name: Genesis 15
references.context: God continues to remind Abram of the promise; descendants remain unfulfilled.

references.name: Genesis 16
references.context: After 11 more years, Abram (age 86) takes matters into his own hands and has a son with Hagar.

references.name: Genesis 17
references.context: Abram and Sarai are renamed Abraham and Sarah; God reiterates the promise that they will have descendants, nations, and kings in covenant with God.

references.name: Genesis 18
references.context: Three visitors appear to Abraham and Sarah, one saying 'I will surely return to you in due season, and your wife Sarah shall have a son.'

references.name: Genesis 21
references.context: Sarah gives birth to Isaac in Abraham's 100th year, 25 years after the initial promise in Genesis 12.

references.name: Abrahamic narrative
references.context: Described as 'the perfect companion for a pandemic, especially a global pandemic that goes on and on and on.'

references.name: Christian tradition (regarding Abraham and Sarah)
references.context: Abraham and Sarah are 'lauded in our Christian tradition for their faithfulness, their steadfastness in waiting on God.'

references.name: Episcopal Church
references.context: References to 'Episcopal parishes in central Denver,' the bishop's Ash Wednesday sermon, and church governance/liturgical practice.

references.name: Lenten tradition
references.context: The sermon is framed within the liturgical calendar of Lent ('the Second Sunday in Lent') and uses Lent as a metaphor for prolonged waiting.

references.name: The bishop's Ash Wednesday sermon (unnamed)
references.context: The preacher references the bishop's description of the pandemic year 'as a year that has tasted like grass,' adopting this metaphorical language.

primary_theme: In the midst of prolonged pandemic waiting, God's faithfulness to promises is demonstrated through Abraham and Sarah's 25-year wait for Isaac, offering comfort and perspective to a congregation frustrated by extended separation and uncertainty.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses the extended Abraham and Sarah narrative as an extended allegory for contemporary pandemic suffering, interspersed with direct pastoral questions to the congregation and practical reassurances about church reopening plans.

references.name: Isaiah (Prophet)
references.context: Isaiah poses rhetorical questions to the Israelites in exile and reminds them of God's creative power and faithfulness.

references.name: Isaiah 40
references.context: The opening questions 'Have you not known? Have you not heard? Has it not been told to you from the beginning?' are quoted from Isaiah 40, addressing exiled Israelites.

references.name: Isaiah 40 (creation imagery)
references.context: God 'stretches out the heavens like a curtain and spreads them like a tent to live in' and 'lift your eyes on high and see.'

references.name: Isaiah 43
references.context: Referenced for the phrase 'God does a new thing,' contrasting return to business as usual with God's creative newness.

references.name: Babylonian Exile
references.context: The historical context of the Israelites' 50-year exile from their homeland, used as the primary interpretive lens for the sermon.

references.name: Unnamed Commentator
references.context: A commentator is cited saying 'exile was hard, but returning will be too,' providing hermeneutical insight into the Isaianic passage.

references.name: Canon Broderick
references.context: Cited from their annual meeting report on the realization that the church 'will always be hybrid,' serving both in-person and remote participants.

references.name: COVID-19 Pandemic (implicit)
references.context: The sermon uses 'exile' as a metaphor for pandemic-related church closures and the shift to online worship, with references to vaccines, distancing, and hybrid models.

references.name: Co-creation theology (implicit)
references.context: The concept of believers becoming 'co-creators with God' reflects process theology and participatory divine action.

primary_theme: As the Israelites learned to move forward rather than merely return from Babylonian exile, post-pandemic churches must embrace creative transformation and become co-creators with God rather than simply restoring what was before.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic challenge; uses historical biblical narrative as hermeneutical lens for contemporary crisis; employs repeated rhetorical questions, lived experience of congregation, and hopeful exhortation rather than scholarly argumentation.

references.name: Genesis 1 (Creation narrative)
references.context: Preacher invokes Genesis as teaching that God 'could bring order out of chaos, who could banish darkness with light.'

references.name: The Baptism of Jesus (Matthew 3, Mark 1, Luke 3, John 1)
references.context: The narrative foundation of the sermon: 'Jesus is baptized by his cousin in the River Jordan. And the heavens were torn apart and the Spirit descended like a dove.'

references.name: The Baptismal Covenant (Episcopal Church liturgy)
references.context: The sermon is structured around the renewal of baptismal vows: questions about 'persevering in resisting evil,' 'proclaiming by word and example the good news,' 'seeking and serving Christ in all persons,' and 'striving for justice and peace.'

references.name: The Book of Common Prayer (1928 version)
references.context: Preacher recalls her own baptism using 'the 1928 version' where she said 'I will, God being my helper.'

references.name: Dismissal formula: 'Remember your baptism'
references.context: Standard Episcopal Church dismissal words that frame the sermon's central exhortation about memory and renewal.

references.name: Sin and Repentance (Christian theology)
references.context: Recurring motif: 'When we fall into sin, not if, when we fall into sin, we will repent and return to the Lord. We fail, we fall, and we try again.'

references.name: Individual and Collective Moral Responsibility (implicit Augustinian/Reformed theology)
references.context: Parallelism drawn between personal moral failure and national failure: 'As it is with the individual, so it is with the collective.'

references.name: Prophetic witness against structural injustice (implicit Liberation Theology)
references.context: Preacher calls attention to systemic racial, social, and medical inequality revealed by the pandemic: 'The disparity in our society is in itself a disease.'

primary_theme: Baptism as a binding covenant that calls us repeatedly to remember our promises to resist evil, seek justice, and serve Christ in others, especially in the face of national moral failure and structural injustice.
rhetorical_style: Prophetic pastoral reflection combining personal narrative (her own baptism story), liturgical recitation (baptismal vows), social critique (listing sites of mass violence and systemic inequality), and exhortation to sustained moral attention rather than premature reconciliation.

references.name: John the Baptist

references.name: Mark (Gospel of Mark)
references.context: Referenced as the source of the description of John the Baptist: 'Mark describes [John], wearing belted camel's hair and eating locust and wild honey while roaming the banks of the Jordan.'

references.name: Isaiah
references.context: Quoted directly at the sermon's conclusion: 'As Mark reminds us this morning, through the words of Isaiah, "Prepare the way of the Lord. Make his paths straight."'

references.name: Jesus Christ / Redeemer
references.context: Referenced in the collect: 'that we may greet with joy the coming of Jesus Christ, our Redeemer.'

references.name: The Collect (liturgical prayer)
references.context: A specific collect for the Second Sunday of Advent is quoted in full: 'Merciful God, who sent your messengers, the prophets, to preach repentance and prepare the way for our salvation...'

references.name: The Prophets (biblical tradition)
references.context: Referenced in the collect as messengers sent by God: 'who sent your messengers, the prophets, to preach repentance and prepare the way for our salvation.'

references.name: Advent (liturgical season and theological concept)
references.context: The entire sermon's framework and primary intellectual scaffold; Advent is described as a time of waiting, preparation, and repentance leading to the coming of Christ.

references.name: Repentance (theological concept)
references.context: Core theme connecting John the Baptist's message, the mother's parenting, and the liturgical season: 'Prepare. Repent. Wait.'

primary_theme: The spiritual discipline of waiting, preparation, and repentance during Advent, illustrated through the preacher's childhood experience of discovering a hidden Christmas gift and learning the value of delayed gratification and restored trust.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling with pastoral reflection, using extended personal anecdote as the primary vehicle for theological instruction, interleaved with liturgical and scriptural references to establish the universal religious significance of the personal lesson.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: The sermon is structured around Matthew's Gospel as the lectionary text for the Twenty-Fourth Sunday after Pentecost, with Matthew 25:14-30 (Parable of the Talents) as the primary text.

references.name: Matthew 25:14-30 (Parable of the Talents)
references.context: The main text: the master entrusts talents to slaves according to their ability; two double their talents while one buries his out of fear.

references.name: Matthew 5 (Sermon on the Mount)
references.context: Referenced when discussing hiding lamps under bushels as an earlier warning in Matthew about not concealing gifts.

references.name: Matthew 13 (Parables of mustard seed and sower)
references.context: Cited as part of the larger narrative pattern in Matthew showing that abundance is meant to be displayed and shared.

references.name: Matthew 5:14-16 (City on a hill)
references.context: Referenced as part of the broader Matthean theme that gifts cannot be hidden but must be visible.

references.name: Matthew 28:19-20 (Great Commission)
references.context: Quoted as the final words of Jesus in Matthew: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Go, therefore, and make disciples of all nations...' Used to reinforce the theme of sharing abundance.

references.name: Faith as a mustard seed
references.context: Used metaphorically when discussing how even small faith or one talent could flourish if not hidden.

references.name: Bishop Frank Logue
references.context: Quoted: 'fear keeps hope locked in a room of doubt' in relation to the third slave's paralysis.

references.name: John Milton - Paradise Lost
references.context: Quoted Satan's words: 'The mind is its own place and in itself can make a heaven of hell, a hell of heaven' to explain how self-imposed exile in outer darkness is created by fear and hiding grace.

references.name: Stewardship theology
references.context: The sermon acknowledges the common stewardship season interpretation of the parable (recognizing gifts and multiplying them for the church) but deliberately rejects it as self-serving.

references.name: Grace and abundance theology
references.context: Central theological framework: God's grace and love are infinite and meant to multiply when shared, not finite when hidden.

references.name: Parable interpretation method
references.context: The preacher notes 'that's the beauty of parables' and deliberately shifts from literal economic interpretation to spiritual/relational interpretation.

primary_theme: God's grace and love are infinite gifts meant to be shared and multiplied openly rather than buried in fear; the parable of the talents teaches that the natural response to trust and abundance is to pay it forward, not pay it back.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic challenge; the preacher uses biblical narrative commentary, theological reinterpretation of a familiar parable, literary allusion, and metaphorical language to redirect listeners from institutional giving toward radical grace-sharing.

references.name: Leviticus
references.context: Referenced as one of two morning readings exhorting God's people to 'love your neighbor as yourself.'

references.name: Matthew (Gospel of Matthew)
references.context: Referenced as one of two morning readings; Matthew 22:37-39 quoted directly with Jesus's answer about the greatest commandment.

references.name: Leviticus 19:18 / Deuteronomy 6:5
references.context: The preacher notes there are 'more than 18 direct messages about loving your neighbor from Leviticus and Deuteronomy through all of the synoptic gospels.'

references.name: Synoptic Gospels (Mark, Luke, Matthew)
references.context: Cited as containing multiple messages about loving one's neighbor.

references.name: Romans
references.context: Cited as containing direct messages about loving your neighbor.

references.name: Galatians
references.context: Cited as containing direct messages about loving your neighbor.

references.name: James (Epistle of James)
references.context: Cited as containing direct messages about loving your neighbor.

references.context: The preacher references the dictionary's website reporting a 30,500% increase in searches for 'schadenfreude' on October 2nd.

references.name: Psychoanalysts and moral philosophers

references.name: German language / etymology
references.context: The preacher discusses how 'schadenfreude is a German word literally translated as damage joy' and explores foreign words integrated into English.

rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with prophetic challenge; combines personal anecdote (family word-love) with concrete contemporary example (schadenfreude spike during presidential COVID diagnosis) to ground scriptural exhortation in real ethical struggle; uses linguistic curiosity to bridge academic and spiritual concerns.

references.context: Reference to the miraculous catch of 153 fish after a night of empty nets, with John as one of the fishermen.

references.name: The Lectionary Tradition

references.context: Referenced as an example of Jesus building community with his disciples.

references.name: Matthew 16:18 ('On this rock I will build my church')

references.context: Declares 'Who do you say that I am?' is 'the seminal question' about what you'll wager your life and identity on.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew

references.name: Matthew 13 (Parables of the Kingdom)

references.context: The primary narrative of the sermon; Jesus feeds 5,000 men plus women and children with five loaves and two fish.

references.name: Nazareth synagogue incident
references.context: Jesus is rejected in his hometown as merely 'the carpenter's son,' leading to the proverb about prophets lacking honor in their own country and house.

references.name: John the Baptist's death (Herod's execution)

references.name: Mark 6:17-29 / Matthew 14:3-12
references.context: The parallel account of John the Baptist's execution, providing narrative context for Jesus's grief at the moment of the feeding miracle.

references.name: Matthew 14:13
references.context: Direct quotation: 'Now when Jesus heard this, he withdrew from there in a boat to a deserted place by himself.'

references.name: Proverb about prophets without honor
references.context: Jesus observes: 'Prophets are not without honor except in their own country and in their own house' (Matthew 13:57).

references.context: Pearson uses the repeated phrase 'The kingdom of heaven is like...' from Matthew's parables to build a theology of the kingdom as enacted through compassion and action.

references.name: Richard (fellow preacher)

references.name: Eucharistic theology / Eucharistic practice
references.context: Implied in the reference to 'Eucharistic visitors' who continue pastoral care during the pandemic, suggesting sacramental theology informing congregational practice.

primary_theme: The kingdom of heaven is not merely taught through parables but enacted through Jesus's and the church's compassionate action, particularly the work of feeding, healing, and tending to the vulnerable, even in times of personal grief and crisis.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling and congregational exhortation; Pearson moves from biblical exposition through theological reflection to contemporary witness of her own parish community during COVID-19, culminating in prophetic encouragement.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Primary text for the sermon; preacher notes Matthew's dualistic worldview ('sheep and goats, wheat and chaff') and explores moments where it allows nuance.

references.name: Matthew 13:1-9 (Parable of the Sower)

references.name: Matthew 13:10-17 (Explanation of the Parable)
references.context: Jesus explains the parable privately to his disciples; passages omitted from the lectionary but referenced as important context.

references.name: Matthew 28 (Great Commission)
references.context: Referenced when describing the disciples' eventual task: 'go out and make disciples of all nations.'

references.name: Gospel of Mark (implied disciples narratives)
references.context: Allusions to James seeking to be Jesus' right-hand man, Peter's denial, and Judas's betrayal.

references.name: Sea of Galilee
references.context: Geographic location where Jesus teaches the parable from a boat.

references.name: Hebrew Scripture/Psalms/Epistles
references.context: Referenced as part of the liturgical reading structure in pre-COVID worship services.

references.name: The Lectionary
references.context: The prescribed Sunday readings; preacher notes that the lectionary omits verses 10-17, the helpful explanatory section of the parable.

references.name: Biblical orality tradition
references.context: The preacher notes that 'the Bible was written to be heard,' emphasizing the oral/performative nature of scripture.

references.name: Liturgical call-and-response ('The word of the Lord / Thanks be to God')
references.context: Traditional Anglican/Episcopal liturgical response; preacher reflects on its new meaning through the lens of the parable.

references.name: Dualistic theology (binary absolutes)

references.context: Preacher places Jesus's compassion alongside traditional titles: Son of God, Child of Humanity, Resurrected One, Vine, Light, Word, Logos (intelligibility of God).

rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with pastoral reflection; opens with an illustrative anecdote (the industrial fan story), moves into scriptural analysis and theological interpretation, then pivots to contemporary application and prophetic challenge for the present moment (COVID-era church).

references.name: John 14:1

references.name: Easter/Resurrection theology

references.name: Christian mysticism (abiding/indwelling)

references.name: Psalm 16 (or similar psalm)

references.name: Committal service liturgy (Anglican/Christian tradition)

references.name: Biblical scholars

references.name: Resurrection practice

references.name: Direct personal encounter with God

The style is accessible, emotionally resonant, and grounded in textual scholarship without being academic.

references.name: Christian Cross Symbolism

references.name: Veneration of the Cross

references.name: Good Friday

references.name: The Sacrifice of Christ / Lord and Savior

references.name: Ordination / Holy Orders
references.context: The preacher mentions being 'made a postulant for ordination' and later becoming a priest, situating her within ordained ministry tradition.

references.name: T.S. Eliot

references.context: Eliot's modernist poem is quoted to illustrate the contrast between glory and mortality, joy and ashes.

references.name: TED Radio Hour podcast on NPR
references.context: Preacher cites a multi-story episode entitled 'Jump-Starting Creativity' featuring talks about how disrupting routine catalyzes creative thinking.

references.name: Episcopal Church tradition
references.context: Preacher references the Episcopal Church custom that 'if we do anything twice in the Episcopal Church, it is considered a tradition.'

references.name: Lenten practice and theology
references.context: Throughout the sermon, Lent is discussed as a liturgical season of abstinence, penitence, and spiritual disruption; covering crosses and images of Christ are mentioned as corporate worship practices.

references.name: Ash Wednesday liturgy
references.context: Preacher references the Ash Wednesday imposition of ashes and a 'litany of penitence' in which the congregation confessed 'indifference to injustice, human need, suffering, and cruelty.'

references.name: The sign of the cross
references.context: Preacher describes 'The sign of the cross was traced in ashes on the foreheads' as part of Ash Wednesday practice.

references.name: Dust theology (memento mori)
references.context: The phrase 'returning to dust' and the emphasis on ashes referencing human mortality and the 'fear in a handful of dust' invokes the medieval tradition of memento mori.

references.name: Grace (Christian theology)
references.context: Preacher speaks of 'deep awareness of our need for grace' and 'the grace of God and the intervention of the Holy Spirit,' central to Christian soteriology.

references.name: Sanctuary (sacred space theology)
references.context: The cathedral is described as 'this literal sanctuary' where reverence and worship occur, invoking the theological concept of sacred space.

rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling blended with contemporary media reference (TED talks) and liturgical reflection, moving from joyful anecdote (pancake races) to somber theological meditation (Ash Wednesday and human suffering), ending with prophetic challenge to reframe Lenten sacrifice as social awareness.

references.name: Matthew (Gospel of Matthew)

references.context: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the law or the prophets. I have come not to abolish, but to fulfill the law. For truly I tell you, until heaven and earth pass away, not one letter, not one stroke of a letter will pass from the law until all is accomplished.'

references.name: The Beatitudes
references.context: Referenced as preceding the antitheses discourse, listing 'The poor in spirit, those who mourn, the pure in heart' as blessed not for following rules but for 'inward orientations of their hearts.'

references.name: The Sermon on the Mount (antitheses section)
references.context: Jesus uses rhetorical device of repeated phrases 'you have heard that it was said, followed closely by the word, but' to deepen compliance with God's will rather than just the letter of the law.

references.name: Matthew on murder/lust/adultery/divorce
references.context: The sermon addresses Matthew's intensification of commandments against killing, lustful gazing, adultery, and divorce as matters of the heart and intention, not merely action.

references.name: Torah/Jewish Law
references.context: Jesus is presented as bringing Torah into deeper compliance 'not with the letter of the law, but with the will of God that inspired the law.'

references.name: The Kingdom of God (reign of God ethic)
references.context: Referenced through a commentator's observation that 'Jesus offers a radical ethic, a reign of God ethic' that emphasizes inward righteousness over rule-following.

references.name: Beatitudes tradition
references.context: Used to illustrate that blessedness comes from 'inward orientations of their hearts' rather than exemplary law-keeping.

references.name: Baptismal covenant
references.context: Invoked to remind listeners they are 'held accountable for our baptismal covenant of honoring the dignity of every human being.'

references.name: Liturgical practice (passing of peace)

references.name: Biblical commentators/scholarship
references.context: One unnamed commentator is cited as positing that 'Jesus offers a radical ethic, a reign of God ethic'; the preacher also references reading 'commentaries and digging down into it' regarding divorce.

references.name: Feminist biblical interpretation
references.context: The preacher highlights Jesus' 'countercultural' and 'feminist tendencies' in not casting women as temptresses but instead holding men accountable for their gaze and objectification.

references.name: Hyperbolic/exaggerated rhetoric
references.context: The preacher notes the 'hyperbolic nature, the exaggeration of Matthew's rhetoric with threats of hellfire, imprisonment, the plucking out of eyes and the chopping off of hands,' implicitly invoking rhetorical criticism.

references.name: Episcopal Church/Anglicanism

references.name: Blessed Sacrament

references.name: Nancy Drew

references.context: The sermon invokes the liturgical tradition of confession, emphasizing 'things done and left undone' as a declaration of need and a cry to be found.
