references.context: The phrase 'but first' is presented as a form of evasion that keeps people from action and discipleship.

primary_theme: In ordinary time, Jesus calls disciples to immediate, wholehearted obedience despite life's hardships, modeling compassion rather than judgment, and sustaining that commitment through trust in God's promises.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling (the Colorado drive, camp experience) combined with scriptural exegesis; uses contemporary application ('much of what we hear today') and invitational re-interpretation of Jesus' tone to challenge congregational assumptions about discipleship.

references.name: Acts 10 (Peter's Vision and the Gentiles)
references.context: Peter is questioned for dining with Gentiles and shares a vision where God shows him that his rules were self-imposed, not God-imposed; God breaks down barriers and the Holy Spirit moves freely.

references.name: The Very Reverend Lodgely Brown Douglas
references.context: Dean of Episcopal Divinity School at Union Theological Seminary who preached at Bishop Kim's consecration and spoke about embracing systemic change in the Episcopal Church.

references.name: Bishop Michael Curry
references.context: Presiding Bishop of the Episcopal Church (referenced in relation to 'royal wedding fame' but acknowledged as far more significant than that single event).

references.name: Dreaming Together (conference/practice)
references.context: A congregational discernment conference held in October 2017 where the community articulated their vision and values for St. John's parish.

references.name: Radical Hospitality
references.context: The preacher calls the congregation to 'show radical hospitality to our own' and to those outside the parish, reflecting a theology of inclusive welcome rooted in the Jesus movement.

references.name: Eucharistic Prayer (liturgical tradition)
references.context: Bishop Kim said a portion of the Eucharistic prayer in Spanish during her consecration, demonstrating inclusive theological practice.

primary_theme: The congregation is called to recognize their identity as a parish with 'embarrassment of riches' in gifts and to extend radical hospitality and inclusion—mirroring Peter's vision in Acts—toward those who are different, marginalized, or new to their community, especially in light of the historic election of their first woman and first African-American bishop.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling blended with prophetic challenge: the preacher uses personal anecdotes and congregational achievement to build community pride, then pivots to exhort the congregation toward prophetic action aligned with the Jesus movement's inclusive mission.

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: Primary biblical text for the sermon; the preacher analyzes John's account of the post-resurrection appearance and Jesus's interaction with Peter.

references.name: John 10:10
references.context: Quoted directly: 'I came that they might have life and have it abundantly.' Used to establish Jesus's identification through acts of abundance.

references.name: Wedding at Cana
references.context: Referenced as an example of Jesus's identification through abundance (the jars and jars of wine).

references.name: Feeding of the 5,000
references.context: Listed as another Johannine example of Jesus identified through abundance with leftovers.

references.name: The miraculous catch of 153 fish
references.context: John 21 account of Peter and disciples fishing; the preacher notes the possible symbolic number 153 and Peter's recognition of Jesus through this abundance.

references.name: Peter's denial of Jesus by the charcoal fire
references.context: Good Friday scene (John 18) that is explicitly linked to the beach scene with the charcoal fire; the preacher uses this to interpret the three questions.

references.name: Mary Magdalene's recognition of Jesus
references.context: Referenced as one way disciples knew Jesus—when he called her name.

references.name: Thomas's recognition of Jesus through wounds
references.context: Cited as another way disciples came to know the risen Jesus.

references.name: Jesus washing Peter's feet
references.context: Alluded to as Peter wanting all of himself washed, not just his feet (John 13).

references.name: Peter cutting off the ear of Malchus
references.context: Referenced as an example of Peter's impetuous nature and character.

references.name: Peter's call to be a fisher of people
references.context: Referenced as Peter's original call when he dropped his net to follow Jesus.

references.name: Gospel commentary tradition
references.context: The preacher explicitly diverges from 'a lot of commentary' on the Peter scene, particularly regarding interpretations of forgiveness and testing.

references.name: Johannine 'I am' statements
references.context: The preacher notes that throughout John, Jesus proclaims 'I am,' contrasted with Peter's declaration 'I am not' on Good Friday.

references.name: Scarcity vs. abundance theology
references.context: The sermon's organizing principle contrasting Peter's scarcity of courage with God's abundance, a framework used throughout contemporary Christian pastoral theology.

primary_theme: After the resurrection, Jesus calls Peter to remember his original calling and to recognize that he is equipped through God's abundance to serve as a disciple, moving beyond the shame and self-doubt of his denial.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative storytelling combined with close exegetical reading; the preacher uses a personal memoir (the bronze sculpture 'Fissure of Men') as an extended metaphor to frame theological reflection on Peter's character and growth.

references.name: John's Gospel
references.context: Jesus's new commandment: 'I give you a new commandment, that you love one another. Just as I have loved you, you also should love one another.'

references.name: John 13 (foot washing and Last Supper)
references.context: Jesus washes his disciples' feet and feeds them with bread and wine in his final hours, exemplifying servant leadership and radical hospitality.

references.name: St. Augustine
references.context: Quoted as observing that the sacred meal reminds us to 'behold what we are so that we may become what we receive.'

references.name: Nadia Boltz-Weber
references.context: A Lutheran pastor cited for the phrase: 'hurt people hurt people, forgiven people forgive people, and loved people love people.'

references.name: Latin phrase 'mandatum novum' (new mandate/commandment)
references.context: The etymology of 'Maundy Thursday' explained as deriving from this Latin phrase, establishing the theological foundation for the sermon.

references.name: Christian Eucharistic theology
references.context: The sacred meal as a sacrament of remembrance and repeated invitation to embody Christ's love despite human failure.

references.name: Servant leadership theology
references.context: Jesus's foot-washing exemplified as the model for Christian service and radical hospitality.

references.name: Liberation theology / theology of the marginalized
references.context: Call to love 'the marginalized, the outcast, the victims of oppression and injustice' as central to Christian mandate.

references.name: Thomas Edison
references.context: Historical figure noted as planting the first royal palms along McGregor Boulevard in Fort Myers in the late 1800s (narrative context, not theological authority).

primary_theme: Jesus's Maundy Thursday mandate to love one another through radical hospitality and service, particularly toward the marginalized, and the repeated invitation to the Eucharistic table as a means of renewal in this calling.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling anchored in personal memory and local place, combined with pastoral reflection on theological obligation and Eucharistic theology, concluding with prophetic challenge to embody Christian love without discrimination.

references.name: Palm Sunday liturgy and observance
references.context: The preacher discusses Palm Sunday's liturgical structure, moving from processional palms ('all glory, laud, and honor') to passion readings ('oh, sacred head, sore wounded').

references.name: Hosanna and Crucifixion narrative
references.context: The sermon's central metaphor: 'We're shouting Hosanna one minute and demanding crucify him moments later,' referencing the crowd's rapid shift from acclamation to condemnation during Holy Week.

references.name: Christian liturgical calendar and seasons
references.context: Referenced through comparison of distinct liturgical moods: 'Think of days like Pentecost or Christmas or Ash Wednesday, and even all of the days that are going to follow this week, culminating in Easter.'

references.name: 'All Glory, Laud, and Honor' (hymn)
references.context: Named explicitly as the opening hymn sung 'across the street' during Palm Sunday's processional.

references.name: 'Oh, Sacred Head, Sore Wounded' (hymn)
references.context: Named explicitly as the closing hymn representing the passion and suffering, 'sore wounded.'

references.name: The Passion of Christ
references.context: Central to the sermon's discussion of Holy Week, referenced as 'the passion' that culminates in the cross and resurrection narrative.

references.name: Easter and the Resurrection
references.context: The theological endpoint referenced as 'that empty tomb' and the culmination of the week's journey 'to the cross and beyond.'

references.name: Musical liturgy and hymnody
references.context: The sermon uses the funeral reception's spliced-together playlist as a hermeneutical key for understanding Palm Sunday's jarring juxtaposition of moods.

primary_theme: Palm Sunday forces us to experience the jarring emotional and spiritual reality of human complexity—rapid shifts from hope to despair, praise to condemnation—which mirrors the mercurial nature of our own lives and calls us to honest self-awareness.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection through extended narrative analogy; the preacher uses the funeral reception anecdote as a controlling metaphor to translate theological truth into accessible emotional and psychological insight, inviting congregational participation and self-recognition rather than scholarly or prophetic pronouncement.

references.name: Cain and Abel
references.context: Listed as an example of poorly presented sibling relationships in biblical narrative used to teach about God and ourselves.

references.name: Jacob and Esau
references.context: Listed alongside Cain and Abel as biblical examples of sibling conflict that illuminate family relationships.

references.name: Joseph
references.context: Referenced as the brother sold into slavery in Egypt by his brothers, exemplifying sibling betrayal in scripture.

references.name: Parable of the Prodigal Son
references.context: The entire sermon centers on exegesis of this parable from Luke 15, analyzing both the younger prodigal son and the elder son's responses to forgiveness.

references.name: Luke 15
references.context: The Gospel account containing the Prodigal Son parable, mentioned as part of Jesus' teachings on the road to Jerusalem.

references.name: The Lord's Prayer
references.context: Quoted directly: 'Forgive us our trespasses as we forgive those who trespass against us'; presented as thematic connection to the parable's emphasis on forgiveness and reconciliation.

references.name: Jesus' teaching to disciples on the road to Jerusalem
references.context: The Lord's Prayer is taught by Jesus to his disciples and followers en route to Jerusalem, immediately preceding the parable in Luke's Gospel.

references.name: Hardness of heart
references.context: Described as a 'wonderful biblical thing' that prevents reconciliation and is suggested as a Lenten practice to relinquish.

references.name: Parable as literary and pedagogical form
references.context: Discussed as a genre that invites multiple character identifications and lacks resolution intentionally to engage the reader/hearer morally.

references.name: Lenten discipline and practice
references.context: Sermon frames forgiveness and reconciliation as appropriate Lenten sacrifices alongside traditional practices like giving up caffeine, sweets, and social media.

references.name: Biblical use of family relationships as theological metaphor
references.context: The sermon explicitly states 'The Bible uses family relationships to teach us much about God and about ourselves,' grounding the interpretive approach.

primary_theme: The Parable of the Prodigal Son calls us beyond celebrating forgiveness to actively practicing reconciliation and releasing bitterness, especially during Lent, by identifying with both the forgiven prodigal and the resentful elder son within ourselves.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection combined with textual exegesis; the preacher uses personal anecdote, character identification prompts, and careful attention to cultural subtext to invite congregational self-examination and moral choice, culminating in imaginative re-engagement with the parable's unresolved ending.

references.name: Genesis 15:5-6
references.context: God tells Abram to look at the stars and count them, promising his descendants will be as numerous; Abram believes and it is reckoned as righteousness.

references.name: Abraham (Old Testament figure)
references.context: Preacher discusses Abraham as the archetypal ancestor and founding father in Genesis, held as the standard by which all others are measured.

references.name: Isaac and Jacob
references.context: Mentioned as part of Abraham's line through which Judaism claims biological and spiritual descent and covenantal relationship with God.

references.name: Ishmael
references.context: Named as Abraham's son through whom Islam traces its spiritual lineage to Muhammad.

references.name: Paul (New Testament)
references.context: Paul is cited as lauding Abraham in the New Testament as a great example of faith.

references.name: Abrahamic Initiative
references.context: Mentioned as working with the State Department to provide interfaith dialogue; presented as an organization with strong relationships with St. John's.

references.name: Interfaith Alliance of Colorado
references.context: Cited alongside Abrahamic Initiative as hosting forums and dialogues with strong relationships to St. John's.

references.name: Baptismal covenant
references.context: The preacher invokes the Episcopal baptismal covenant to challenge the congregation to strive for justice and peace and respect the dignity of every human being.

references.name: The three great monotheistic religions (Judaism, Christianity, Islam)
references.context: Abraham is presented as the shared father figure and symbol of possibility for peace and cooperation among these three world religions.

references.name: Christ Church New Zealand (Christchurch mosque shooting)
references.context: Referenced as 'the news out of Christ Church New Zealand' to which the preacher awoke on Friday morning; this is an allusion to the March 15, 2019 Christchurch terrorist attack.

references.name: Charleston church shooting
references.context: Referenced implicitly when preacher states 'Every person should feel safe in prayer and worship, whether in a church in Charleston'—alluding to the 2015 Charleston church massacre.

references.name: Pittsburgh synagogue shooting
references.context: Referenced when preacher states 'a synagogue in Pittsburgh'—alluding to the October 2018 Pittsburgh synagogue attack.

references.name: Racism and white supremacy
references.context: Named as an 'insidious disease' that must stop; positioned as the underlying cause of the faith-based atrocities referenced in the sermon.

primary_theme: Using Abraham as a shared spiritual ancestor of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, the preacher calls for interfaith dialogue, justice-seeking action, and the rejection of racism and white supremacy in response to religiously motivated violence.
rhetorical_style: Narrative storytelling combined with prophetic challenge; the preacher opens with a personal anecdote about Muslim teenagers visiting the cathedral, then moves to biblical grounding and concludes with direct calls to action, explicitly rejecting passive 'thoughts and prayers' in favor of concrete engagement and relationship-building.

references.name: Mary Oliver
references.context: Referenced as a poet who saw holiness in nature and hid pencils in trees to record observations; her death prompted reflections on attentiveness and astonishment.

references.name: Mary Oliver, 'Sometimes'
references.context: Quoted directly: 'Pay attention. Be astonished. Tell about it' — identified as encapsulating the gospel message and epiphany in three lines.

references.name: Mary Oliver, 'Mysteries, Yes'
references.context: Quoted at sermon's close: 'Truly we live with mysteries too marvelous to be understood' and 'Let me keep company always with those who say, look, laugh in astonishment, and bow their heads.'

references.name: Gospel of John
references.context: Referenced for its terminology of 'signs' rather than 'miracles' to describe Jesus's acts; entire theological framework of the sermon built on John's usage.

references.name: John 2:1-11 (Wedding at Cana)
references.context: Extended narrative analysis of Jesus's first sign: water turned to wine, Mary's intervention ('Do whatever he tells you'), and the provision of 120-180 gallons of wine as sign of abundance.

references.name: John 4:46-54 (Official's son)
references.context: Referenced as example of sign: 'Go, Jesus will tell the official, and your son will live. And he lived.'

references.name: John 5:1-9 (Healing of paralytic)
references.context: Cited as sign: 'Stand up, Jesus said to the paralytic. Take your mat and walk. And he walked.'

references.name: John 11:1-44 (Lazarus)
references.context: Referenced as sign: 'Lazarus, Jesus called to his friend, come out. And he came out.'

references.name: Gospel tradition (Epiphany season)
references.context: Sermon set within liturgical season of Epiphany; references the manifestation of Christ, connection to pre-Christian rituals, and seasonal cycles of light.

references.name: Christian incarnational theology
references.context: Preacher argues against 'shiny, clean, heaven-only faith' and emphasizes encountering God in 'the earthy, seasonal, and deeply human elements' and material world.

references.name: Psalm 34:8
references.context: Implicit reference: 'It is in the earthy that we taste and see and truly experience the manifestation of God' echoes 'taste and see that the Lord is good.'

references.name: Contemplative/mystical tradition
references.context: Emphasis on attentiveness, astonishment, presence to mystery, and 'being detectives of the divine' reflects apophatic mystical theology valuing wonder over certainty.

primary_theme: During the Epiphany season, God's divine presence is revealed through signs in nature and the ordinary world, calling us to practice attentiveness and astonishment rather than claiming certainty.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with poetic sensibility; uses narrative examples (Mary Oliver's biography, Gospel stories) and lyrical quotation to build a contemplative argument about encountering the divine in seasonal cycles and material reality.

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Episcopal priest, lecturer, and professor cited as author of 'An Altar in the World' and the concept of being 'a detective of the divine.'

references.name: An Altar in the World
references.context: Barbara Brown Taylor's work exploring the practice of finding God anywhere and everywhere, not just in traditional church spaces.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: Matthew 2:1-12 - the only biblical reference to the Magi; preacher emphasizes what Matthew did NOT say about them.

references.name: Micah (prophecy)
references.context: Chief priests and scribes knew the words of Micah that foretold a ruler of Judah coming from Bethlehem.

references.name: Hebrew Scripture
references.context: Noted that the Magi had no knowledge of Hebrew scripture, yet still found Jesus.

references.name: Book of Common Prayer
references.context: Referenced as part of familiar Episcopal worship practices, alongside liturgical actions.

references.name: Nancy Drew
references.context: Preacher mentions always wanting to be Nancy Drew and compares Epiphany season to detective work ('detective of the divine').

references.name: Episcopal/Anglican theological tradition
references.context: Preacher reflects on particular Episcopal ways of experiencing God through formality, ritual, and liturgical practice.

references.name: Medieval/Christian mystical tradition
references.context: Implicit in Taylor's reference to encountering God in 'whirlwinds, starry skies, burning bushes'—echoing medieval mystical theology of divine encounter.

primary_theme: God encounters and uses unlikely people (the Magi) to reveal divine truth, challenging narrow perceptions of where and how God is found and calling believers to be flexible, obedient detectives of the divine in unexpected places.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling; combines personal memoir (childhood fascination with Magi, Episcopal practices), textual analysis (close reading of Matthew), and literary allusion (Barbara Brown Taylor, Nancy Drew) to invite congregation into theological reflection and spiritual practice.

references.name: Gospel of John (Prologue)
references.context: The sermon centers on John 1:1-18, particularly the opening 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God,' and the closing verses about grace upon grace.

references.name: John 1:1
references.context: Quoted directly: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.'

references.name: John 1:16-18
references.context: The final lines of John's prologue quoted: 'From his fullness, we have all received grace upon grace. The law indeed was given through Moses. Grace and truth came through Jesus Christ. No one has ever seen God. It is God, the only Son, who is close to the Father's heart, who has made him known.'

references.name: Richard Hooker
references.context: An Anglican theologian cited as emphasizing 'the gratuitous nature of grace and the importance of our participation in God's salvation through it.'

references.name: Book of Common Prayer (Catechism)
references.context: The catechism section is cited as defining grace: 'God's favor towards us, unearned and undeserved,' and describing grace's effects: 'By grace, God forgives our sins, enlightens our minds, stirs our hearts, and strengthens our wills.'

references.name: Anglican Liturgical Tradition
references.context: References to the Holy Eucharist/sacrament as the means through which grace is made known 'week after week in the life of the Church' and communal rites as channels of grace.

references.name: Christian doctrine of grace (soteriological)
references.context: The sermon develops a traditional Christian understanding of grace as God's unearned favor for human salvation, particularly as embodied in Jesus Christ.

references.name: Lessons and Carols liturgical observance
references.context: The John prologue was heard 'both Saturday and Sunday of Lessons and Carols' in the preceding weeks.

primary_theme: God's grace, revealed in Jesus Christ and exemplified in the incarnation, is an unearned gift of divine love that should shape how Christians respond to themselves and others, particularly as we enter a new calendar year.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling (the anecdote of the mother and children in the grocery store) combined with liturgical/catechetical instruction, inviting congregational self-examination through rhetorical questions and practical application rather than scholarly argument.

references.name: Book of Jeremiah
references.context: "Jeremiah tells us the days are coming" - used to illustrate the theme of waiting and future promise.

references.name: Psalms
references.context: "Four times the psalmist implores God to teach them" - depicts the psalmist in a state of waiting between ignorance and knowledge.

references.name: 1 Thessalonians
references.context: "The letter to the Thessalonians prays that the people may be blameless at the coming of our Lord" - illustrates waiting for Christ's return.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: "Luke's gospel tells us that there will be signs and that the Son of Man will come in a cloud with power and great glory" and "Luke also warns us to be on guard and to be alert at all times."

references.name: The Nicene Creed (Eucharistic prayer formulation)
references.context: "words that we say almost weekly in the 8 a.m. Eucharistic prayer" - the affirmation "Christ has died. Christ is risen. Christ will come again."

references.name: C.S. Lewis - The Chronicles of Narnia
references.context: "It was that Narnia feeling of always winter and never Christmas" - allusion to the perpetual winter in Narnia as metaphor for her Minnesota experience.

references.name: Christian Advent Tradition
references.context: The entire sermon centers on Advent as a liturgical season that reminds Christians of eschatological waiting and the in-between time of Christ's absence and future return.

references.name: Christian Eschatology
references.context: "As Christians, we exist in this in-between time. Christ has died. Christ is risen. Christ will come again, but has not yet." - the theological framework of already/not-yet eschatology.

references.name: Contemplative/Mindfulness Practice (implicit)
references.context: The emphasis on "paying attention to the present," "noticing the small things," and "the changing of the light each day" reflects contemplative spiritual traditions emphasizing presence and mindfulness.

primary_theme: During Advent, Christians must learn to wait patiently for Christ's return while remaining fully present and attentive to the blessings and details of the present moment, rather than wishing time away in pursuit of future desires.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral narrative reflection using personal testimony (the Minnesota winters and mother's death) as the primary vehicle for theological instruction, combined with scriptural grounding and gentle prophetic challenge against commercialism and distraction.

references.name: Psalm 19:14
references.context: Opening invocation: 'May these words of my mouth and this meditation of all of our hearts be pleasing in your sight, Lord our rock and our redeemer.'

references.name: John 11 (Lazarus narrative)
references.context: The core biblical text for the sermon; discusses Mary and Martha's encounter with Jesus regarding their brother Lazarus's death and resurrection.

references.name: John 11:21 (paraphrased)
references.context: Mary's lament quoted: 'Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died.' Preacher notes these words transcend time and are echoed by modern grievers.

references.name: Baptism theology (sacramental)
references.context: In baptism, 'we too are buried and resurrected with Christ,' connecting Lazarus's resurrection to Christian sacramental participation.

references.name: Eucharist theology
references.context: In the Eucharist, 'we are united with Christ and with all those we love, but see no longer,' expressing communion of saints doctrine.

references.name: Communion of Saints
references.context: Central theological concept: 'We are all, in all ways and at all times, sharing in the risen life of Christ with each other, with all those who have come before us, and all those who will come after.'

references.name: All Saints' Day / All Souls' Day liturgy
references.context: The observance framing the sermon; reflects on the church's practice of honoring the dead and the communion between living and deceased believers.

references.name: Lent Madness (2019 bracket)
references.context: Referenced as a 'fun and lighthearted' church tradition that models March Madness for college basketball, featuring saints in competitive brackets.

references.name: King James Version (Bible translation)
references.context: Preacher cites KJV phrasing of Martha's concern about Lazarus: 'Lazarus surely stinketh,' praising the translation's practical language.

references.name: Winston Churchill
references.context: Historical figure cited for planning his own funeral service in detail at St. Paul's Cathedral, London, called 'Operation Hope Not.'

references.name: Archbishop of Canterbury
references.context: Referenced as the figure who blessed those gathered at Churchill's funeral and led the singing of the national anthem.

references.name: St. Paul's Cathedral, London
references.context: Named as the location of Winston Churchill's funeral service, illustrating the grandeur of liturgical preparation and the church's role in marking life transitions.

references.name: Swanee's All Saints Chapel (University of the South)
references.context: Preacher recalls walking past this chapel as a student and later as a mature seminary student, creating a personal connection across time to memory and spiritual formation.

references.name: Thin place / thin places (Celtic spirituality / mystical tradition)
references.context: Preacher describes her experience on All Souls Walk as 'an experience of a thin place. A moment that was both in time and out of time.'

references.name: Linear time vs. Christian time
references.context: Preacher contrasts cultural obsession with clocks, calendars, and deadlines with a distinctly Christian understanding of time as non-linear and sacramental.

references.name: March Madness (college basketball)
references.context: Referenced as the secular model for Lent Madness, a church-based competition featuring saints.

references.name: Sacramental theology (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's entire arc assumes a sacramental worldview in which the church building, liturgy, and sacraments are conduits of grace and the presence of Christ.

primary_theme: Christian time is non-linear and sacramental; in the communion of saints through baptism and Eucharist, the living are united with the dead and with Christ, transcending temporal boundaries and grief.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection using personal narrative and storytelling (preacher's own struggle with punctuality, memory of seminary chapel, All Souls Walk experience) to develop theological insight; combines intimate vulnerability with liturgical-sacramental theology; uses humor and contemporary culture references (Lent Madness, Denver traffic) to make profound spiritual concepts accessible.

references.name: William Porcher DuBose
references.context: DuBose was the first chaplain of Sewanee (1871), later professor and dean of the seminary; widely considered one of the most important Episcopal theologians who developed systematic theology and was ahead of his time on ecumenism.

references.name: DuBose's Ecumenical Theology
references.context: DuBose believed that no single church has the full picture of Christ, and that different churches understand different aspects of Christ; together a fuller picture emerges—a revolutionary idea that rejected sectarian claims to sole truth.

references.name: Gospel of Mark (Mark 9:38-50 implied)
references.context: The primary text referenced: John reports an exorcist casting out demons in Jesus' name but not following the disciples; Jesus rebukes John for trying to exclude the outsider and warns against putting up barriers before believers.

references.name: Mark 9:33-37 (implied previous week's gospel)
references.context: Jesus calls a child into the midst of the disciples in the context of their argument over who is greatest; the child remains on Jesus' lap as he speaks about not putting barriers before little ones.

references.name: Dualistic thinking / binary thought
references.context: The preacher critiques the contemporary tendency toward binary thinking ('if I am right, then you must be wrong'; 'black, white, in, out, right, wrong') and argues for holding opposing views in healthy tension.

references.name: Both-and vs. Either-or thinking
references.context: The preacher argues that 'it is rarely, if ever, an either-or. It is most often both-and,' suggesting familiarity with dialectical or paradoxical theological reasoning common in modern theology.

references.name: Ecumenism
references.context: The preacher emphasizes ecumenical work as essential to widening the circle of understanding Christ; references both formal ecumenical education (ILIF) and grassroots ecumenical engagement within congregations.

references.name: ILIF (Institute for Leadership in Ministry or similar)
references.context: An educational program where the preacher studied scripture with classmates from varied faith traditions, which deepened her understanding of her own beliefs.

references.name: Catechumenate
references.context: An ancient church tradition of instruction for new believers; the preacher co-teaches this with multiple instructors of differing approaches, modeling the both-and theology being advocated.

references.name: Stumbling block (biblical concept)
references.context: Jesus implies that John is being a 'stumbling block' by excluding others; the preacher applies this to contemporary church practice, warning that exclusive claims can be barriers to others who believe.

references.name: The circle of the beloved
references.context: Jesus is described as 'opening and expanding the circle of the beloved' and 'widening the circle,' language that suggests inclusive theology and boundary-crossing ethics common in liberation and progressive theology.

primary_theme: Following Jesus requires rejecting exclusive, dualistic thinking and embracing an expansive, ecumenical theology that recognizes Christ is seen more fully when diverse Christian communities and perspectives are welcomed rather than excluded.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with scholarly grounding: The preacher combines personal anecdotes (alma mater experience, co-teaching), historical/theological scholarship (DuBose), biblical exegesis (Mark 9), and contemporary cultural critique (dualistic society in 2018) to build an argument for theological humility and ecumenical openness. The tone is conversational and warm, with moments of humor, while the argument is systematically developed through reference to both tradition and present practice.

references.name: Isaiah
references.context: Jesus quotes the prophet Isaiah: 'This people honor me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me.'

references.name: Isaiah 29:13
references.context: The specific prophetic passage Jesus invokes to rebuke external piety without internal transformation.

references.name: Mark 7:1-23 (or parallel Gospel account)
references.context: The Fifteenth Sunday after Pentecost gospel reading about Pharisees, scribes, hand-washing traditions, and what defiles a person.

references.name: Hebrew scriptures concept of 'heart' (leb)
references.context: The Hebrews understood the heart as the governing center encompassing physical, intellectual, and psychological attributes of the whole person.

references.name: New Testament concept of 'heart'
references.context: In the New Testament, the heart is positioned as the seat of intellect and will, distinguishing it from emotional centers lower in the anatomy.

references.name: Paul

references.context: Dove descending and voice from heaven proclaiming 'You are my son, with you I am well pleased' - demonstrates dramatic irony of the gospel.

references.name: Shakespeare
references.context: Cited as using dramatic irony repeatedly in his plays (example: Juliet and Cesario references).

references.name: Shakespeare's plays (implied: Romeo and Juliet, Twelfth Night)
references.context: Used as literary comparison for dramatic irony technique: 'Juliet isn't really dead, and Cesario is not a good love match for Olivia.'

references.name: Matt Skinner
references.context: New Testament professor cited for commentary on the text appearing in the season of Epiphany and Christ becoming visible and known to the world.

references.name: Epiphany (liturgical season theology)
references.context: Invoked as theological framework for understanding Christ's manifestation and visibility in the world; used to frame contemporary application.

references.name: Literary analysis of scripture
references.context: Entire hermeneutical approach examining Mark as literature with devices like dramatic irony, urgency, narrative structure, and thematic unity.

references.name: Historical-critical biblical interpretation
references.context: Framework for reading scripture as history with consideration of original audience, cultural context, and written tradition.

references.name: Scripture as revelation of God's relationship through the Trinity
references.context: Presented as one hermeneutical lens alongside historical and literary approaches for interpreting scripture.

references.name: Oral tradition and oral hearing of gospels
references.context: Noted that Mark's gospel would take 1.5 hours to hear in Greek and was experienced by largely illiterate audiences, informing understanding of narrative devices.

primary_theme: Jesus' authority and identity are revealed through Mark's carefully crafted literary devices and dramatic irony, inviting contemporary audiences to be amazed by and open to Jesus' power in their own lives.
rhetorical_style: Scholarly literary analysis combined with pastoral application; uses close textual reading, comparative literature references, and liturgical-theological reflection to bridge ancient text with contemporary faith practice.

references.name: The Magnificat (Song of Mary)
references.context: The central biblical text; preacher reads and interprets Luke 1:46-55 throughout the sermon as Mary's prophetic declaration of God's justice.

references.name: Luke 1:46-55
references.context: The full Magnificat passage quoted multiple times, including 'He has shown the strength of his arm. He has scattered the proud in their conceit. He has cast down the mighty from their thrones and has lifted up the lowly. He has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich he has sent away empty.'

references.name: Luke 2 (Simeon in the Temple)
references.context: Preacher references when 'Simeon tells her in the temple of all that is to come in Jesus' life' as evidence Mary knew of her son's suffering and sacrifice.

references.name: Geoffrey Chaucer
references.context: Listed as a medieval writer who contributed to the 'corpus of Marian literature' and the literary tradition shaping cultural understanding of Mary.

references.name: William Wordsworth
references.context: Named as a poet who contributed to the Marian literary tradition that has shaped cultural portrayals of Mary.

references.name: Henry Wadsworth Longfellow
references.context: Named as a poet (along with Wordsworth and Chaucer) whose writings on Mary have contributed to the 'corpus of Marian literature.'

references.name: Will.I.Am (hip-hop artist)
references.context: Referenced as the rapper whose song 'I Got It from My Mama' became an earworm that the preacher uses as a metaphorical lens for understanding Mary's role in shaping Jesus' ministry.

references.name: Medieval Marian spirituality and devotion
references.context: Preacher identifies 'Devotion to Mary was central to medieval spirituality and writing' as a historical tradition that shaped later literary and artistic portrayals of Mary.

references.name: The incarnation/incarnational theology
references.context: Preacher references 'this incarnational moment' as the point where Christians can 'most deeply understand and connect with Jesus' through his full humanity as a baby.

references.name: Marian literature (corpus of)
references.context: Preacher discusses how Mary has 'played a dominant role in literature and art throughout the ages' and how this shapes modern understanding more than scripture itself.

references.name: Biblical prophecy/prophetic tradition
references.context: Preacher identifies Mary as 'a prophet' through her Magnificat and suggests she 'prophesies the ministry of Jesus' before he becomes an adult.

references.name: Liberation theology motifs (implicit)
references.context: The sermon's emphasis on God casting down the mighty, lifting up the lowly, filling the hungry, and Jesus as a disruptor of unjust power structures reflects liberation theological themes.

references.name: Feminist reinterpretation of Mary (implicit)
references.context: Preacher challenges the 'passive, pliant, young, naive' portrayal of Mary and calls for a 'grittier,' 'scrappier' image of Mary as an active agent and partner with God, reflecting contemporary feminist biblical hermeneutics.

references.name: The 'cult of Mary' or Marian devotion imagery (implicit)
references.context: Preacher critiques 'the polished, pristine, and pedestaled Mary' as a culturally constructed image rather than a biblically grounded one.

primary_theme: Mary is not a passive, silent vessel but an active, prophetic figure whose Magnificat reveals her as a woman of justice and resistance who intentionally shaped Jesus' ministry through teaching him God's work of liberation for the oppressed.
rhetorical_style: Personal pastoral reflection combined with cultural and literary analysis; the preacher uses a contemporary pop song hook as an entry point to challenge inherited literary and artistic traditions about Mary, then pivots to close textual reading of Scripture to recover a more active and prophetic Mary than popular devotion typically portrays.

references.name: Matthew's Gospel
references.context: The sermon centers on Matthew 25:31-46, the parable of the sheep and the goats used as the primary text for final judgment and community care.

references.name: Pentecost
references.context: Referenced as the starting point of 'ordinary time,' the liturgical season spanning from Pentecost to Christ the King Sunday.

references.name: Mark's Gospel
references.context: Mentioned as the gospel that will replace Matthew in the liturgical cycle next Sunday, ushering in Advent.

references.name: Advent
references.context: The coming liturgical season characterized by the theme of Emmanuel, 'God with us,' and preparation for Christmas.

references.name: Christ the King Sunday
references.context: The liturgical occasion being observed, marking the last Sunday of ordinary time and the church's new year.

references.name: Ignatian Spirituality / Daily Examen
references.context: The preacher invites the congregation to practice the Ignatian Daily Examen, a five-step reflective prayer discipline from the Jesuit tradition.

references.name: Ignatius of Loyola (implied)
references.context: The Daily Examen is attributed to Ignatian spirituality, though Ignatius is not named directly; referenced through 'Ignatian spiritual practice.'

references.name: Loyola Press
references.context: Cited as the source for the description of the five-step Daily Examen process.

references.name: Elf on the Shelf
references.context: Used as a cultural reference to critique surveillance-based morality, contrasting fear-based judgment with grace-based presence.

references.name: Liturgical Year / Church Calendar
references.context: The sermon emphasizes the cyclical nature of the liturgical calendar and the practice of repetition as spiritual grounding.

references.name: Parable tradition (Matthew 24-25)
references.context: The preacher references a series of parables about preparedness: the wise and foolish bridesmaids, faithful and lazy slaves, and properly dressed wedding guests.

references.name: Emmanuel / God with us
references.context: Referenced as the central message of Advent, emphasizing Christ's presence throughout all seasons.

references.name: Dualism
references.context: The preacher identifies a dualistic structure in Matthew's parables: wise/foolish, good/wicked, trusted/untrustworthy.

references.name: Community care / Neighborly love
references.context: The sermon's core argument draws on the Christian tradition of loving one's neighbor through concrete acts of service and community building.

references.name: Shepherd/sheep metaphor
references.context: Used throughout to represent Jesus as guide and model; sheep as followers who emulate Christ's example of care.

primary_theme: In the parable of sheep and goats, Jesus calls followers to emulate his example of community care and neighborly service, not to earn divine favor but as a natural expression of having internalized his teachings; the Daily Examen offers a practical spiritual practice for reflecting on whether we have acted as 'sheep' or 'goats' and for growing in love and community building.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection with narrative storytelling; the preacher uses personal anecdote (Lancaster, Pennsylvania farms), contemporary cultural reference (Elf on the Shelf, YouTube goat videos), humor, and gentle self-disclosure (mentioning her spiritual director) to make theological concepts accessible; concludes with a practical spiritual discipline invitation, creating both intellectual engagement and pastoral warmth.

references.name: Barbara Brown Taylor
references.context: Pearson quotes Taylor's preference for Luke's version of the parable over Matthew's: 'if Matthew and Luke had churches in my town, I would definitely go to Luke's church.'

references.name: Jerome Berryman
references.context: Pearson references Berryman as the creator of the Godly Play program and mentions spending time with him during the week of the sermon.

references.name: Godly Play Program
references.context: Pearson describes Godly Play as an educational program used at St. John's and in over 40 countries that teaches biblical stories through imaginative engagement with specific contemplative questions.

references.name: Gospel of Matthew
references.context: The sermon discusses Matthew's version of the wedding banquet parable, particularly Matthew's harsher ending not found in Luke.

references.name: Gospel of Luke
references.context: Pearson references Luke's parallel version of the wedding banquet parable, which ends more happily without Matthew's judgment of the underdressed guest.

references.name: Matthew 22 (Wedding Banquet Parable)
references.context: The primary biblical text examined throughout the sermon, including the parable of the king, the wedding feast, the rejection of invitations, and the judgment of the improperly dressed guest.

references.name: Old Testament
references.context: Pearson mentions that the preaching team has focused on Old Testament readings in recent weeks rather than gospel passages.

references.name: Pauline Epistles
references.context: Pearson notes that the preaching team has treated the epistles in recent Sundays rather than focusing on Matthew's gospel.

references.name: Parable Tradition
references.context: Pearson discusses parables as a literary and pedagogical form in the Gospels and references Richard's teaching that 'parables are an invitation to imagine.'

references.name: Imaginative/Contemplative Biblical Engagement
references.context: The sermon's entire methodology draws on a tradition of inviting readers to imaginatively and contemplatively engage with scripture rather than seek singular doctrinal meanings, consistent with modern narrative theology approaches.

references.name: Inclusive Theology/God's Universal Invitation
references.context: Pearson emphasizes God's inclusive nature and persistent invitation to all people regardless of status, reflecting liberation theology and inclusive Christianity perspectives.

primary_theme: Through imaginative engagement with Matthew's parable of the wedding banquet, we are invited to recognize God's persistent and inclusive invitation to all people and our responsibility to actively accept and respond to that invitation with full engagement and transformation.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with narrative storytelling, using dialogue with other theological voices (Barbara Brown Taylor), pedagogical methodology (Godly Play questions), and personal wondering to lead the congregation toward active spiritual participation.

references.name: Book of Jonah
references.context: The entire sermon is an exegesis of the Book of Jonah, examining Jonah as an antihero character and exploring the irony of his resistance to God's mercy.

references.name: Jonah 1-4 (Jonah running, being swallowed by fish, arriving in Nineveh, becoming angry at God's mercy)
references.context: Multiple detailed references to the narrative arc: Jonah fleeing God's call, being cast into the sea, surviving in the fish, preaching to Nineveh, and his anger at God's compassion.

references.name: Gospel lesson (Parable of the Laborers in the Vineyard)
references.context: Cited as parallel to Jonah story: workers hired at different times receive equal pay, illustrating that 'that's just not the way grace works.'

references.name: Matthew 20:1-16 (implied)
references.context: The parable of the laborers in the vineyard where morning workers resent those hired later receiving equal wages.

references.name: Holden Caulfield
references.context: Listed as a familiar antihero character alongside Huck Finn, Scarlett O'Hara, Jay Gatsby, and Severus Snape—deeply flawed characters who often do the right thing for wrong reasons.

references.name: The Catcher in the Rye (J.D. Salinger)
references.context: Implicitly referenced through the character Holden Caulfield as an example of literary antiheroes.

references.name: Huck Finn
references.context: Listed as a familiar antihero character who is deeply flawed yet often does the right thing, sometimes for wrong reasons.

references.name: The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (Mark Twain)
references.context: Implicitly referenced through the character Huck Finn as an example of literary antiheroes.

references.name: Scarlett O'Hara
references.context: Listed as a familiar antihero character who is deeply flawed yet often does the right thing, sometimes for wrong reasons.

references.name: Gone with the Wind (Margaret Mitchell)
references.context: Implicitly referenced through the character Scarlett O'Hara as an example of literary antiheroes.

references.name: Jay Gatsby
references.context: Listed as a familiar antihero character who is deeply flawed yet often does the right thing, sometimes for wrong reasons.

references.name: The Great Gatsby (F. Scott Fitzgerald)
references.context: Implicitly referenced through the character Jay Gatsby as an example of literary antiheroes.

references.name: Severus Snape
references.context: Listed as a familiar antihero character from contemporary literature who is deeply flawed yet often does the right thing, sometimes for wrong reasons.

references.name: Harry Potter series (J.K. Rowling)
references.context: Implicitly referenced through the character Severus Snape as an example of literary antiheroes.

references.name: Antihero literary tradition
references.context: The sermon draws on the literary tradition of antiheroes as a framework for understanding Jonah: 'Antiheroes abound in literature' and they are 'more complex and interesting than those characters who are perfect pillars of society.'

references.name: Presbyterian theology (implicit reference to Jonah's Presbyterian upbringing)
references.context: The preacher notes that a Presbyterian child would receive the message that 'no matter what you did or where you went, you could not hide from God,' contrasting with her own Episcopal upbringing.

references.name: Episcopal theology (implicit reference to the preacher's Episcopal upbringing)
references.context: The preacher reflects that as an Episcopal girl she 'wasn't afraid, nor was I having very deep theological thoughts as a child,' suggesting a different theological emphasis than Presbyterian tradition.

references.name: Bitterness as poison
references.context: The preacher cites her father's expression: 'Bitterness is like drinking poison and waiting for the other person to die,' applied to Jonah's inability to rejoice in God's mercy to others.

references.name: Narrative formula of biblical prophecy
references.context: The sermon discusses how standard biblical prophets are reluctant but quickly obedient, their messages lead to destruction, they wish to die, and they are narrators not main characters—all of which Jonah inverts.

references.name: Children's literature and picture books
references.context: The preacher reflects on how the Jonah story is presented differently in children's books versus its full biblical complexity, emphasizing obedience and fantastical imagery.

references.name: Psalm 19:14 (implied)
references.context: Opening invocation: 'May these words of my mouth and this meditation of all of our hearts be pleasing in your sight, Lord our Rock and our Redeemer.'

references.name: Jonah 4:2 (direct quote)
references.context: Jonah's complaint to God: 'I knew that you were a gracious God and merciful and slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love and ready to relent from punishing.'

primary_theme: Jonah is presented as a biblical antihero whose story teaches that God's grace and mercy are extended to all people regardless of apparent deservingness, challenging us to examine where we withhold grace from others and cling to bitterness instead.
rhetorical_style: Pastoral reflection combined with literary analysis and personal narrative; the preacher uses her own childhood memories, her father's wisdom, and parallel literary examples to make the theological argument accessible and emotionally resonant while inviting congregational self-examination.

references.name: John Ortberg
references.context: Evangelical pastor and author cited for his bestselling early-2000s book on the walking-on-water story, promoting a self-help theology of personal achievement through trust.

references.name: If You Want to Walk on Water, You've Got to Get Out of the Boat
references.context: Ortberg's bestseller that frames the Peter story as about personal accomplishment and stepping out in faith; Pearson critiques this interpretation as prosperity gospel.

references.name: Dietrich Bonhoeffer
references.context: Invoked for the assertion that 'we see Jesus most clearly in the face of another when we engage in life together,' supporting the community-centered interpretation.

references.name: Matthew 8:23-27 (storm-calming story)
references.context: Referenced as a parallel account where disciples in a boat ask 'what sort of man is this' after Jesus calms the storm.

references.name: Matthew 14:22-33 (walking on water)
references.context: The primary text being preached; the story of Peter attempting to walk on water while other disciples remain in the boat.

references.name: Matthew 4:1-11 (Satan's temptation of Jesus in wilderness)
references.context: Alluded to when Pearson notes Peter's challenge 'If you are the Son of God, command...' parallels Satan's temptation formula.

references.name: Children's Bible stories tradition
references.context: Referenced as the source of the familiar, dramatized version of the walking-on-water story with 'sound effect words for roaring wind and crashing waves.'

references.name: Prosperity gospel
references.context: Pearson identifies Ortberg's theology as smacking of prosperity gospel—the idea that sufficient prayer, trust, and righteous living leads to blessing and favor.

references.name: American individualism
references.context: Pearson contrasts Peter's individual action with the disciples' communal faithfulness, suggesting Peter exemplifies an American ideal of self-reliance.

references.name: Biblical emojis (implicit reference to semiotics/tone)
references.context: Playful reference to the problem of interpreting written biblical text without oral/tonal cues, acknowledging the hermeneutical gap between email/text and ancient manuscripts.

primary_theme: The preaching of Christian discipleship values communal faithfulness and staying together through storms over individualistic achievement and personal risk-taking, reinterpreting the walking-on-water story to celebrate the disciples who remained in the boat rather than Peter's leap of faith.
rhetorical_style: Narrative-driven pastoral reflection combined with textual critique; Pearson uses personal anecdote (Clara's story), lived community experience (her month at St. John's), careful exegetical observation (tone, context, parallel passages), and scholarly reference to build a counter-cultural interpretation against established evangelical readings.
